∴ Thinking…

  Let me trace through this logic:

  1. In grub_cmd_normal (line 320), if grub_menu_title is not set, it initializes it with "GNU GRUB  version X.Y.Z" and exports it.
  2. In grub_normal_init_page (line 209), it gets the title from the environment variable instead of formatting it directly.There's a
  potential issue:Old code (lines 217-220):
  msg_formatted = grub_xasprintf (_("GNU GRUB  version %s"), PACKAGE_VERSION);
  if (!msg_formatted)
    return;
  ```If grub_xasprintf failed, it would return NULL, and we'd return early.

  New code (lines 218-220):```c
  msg_title = grub_env_get ("grub_menu_title");if (! msg_title)
    return;

  If grub_env_get returns NULL, we return early. This is fine.

  But wait, let me check the initialization code (lines 323-332):```c
  if (grub_env_get ("grub_menu_title") == NULL)
    {    /* Initialize the menu title with a GRUB version information. */
      char *title;    title = grub_xasprintf (_("GNU GRUB  version %s"), PACKAGE_VERSION);
      grub_env_set ("grub_menu_title", title);
      grub_free (title);    grub_env_export ("grub_menu_title");
    }

  If grub_xasprintf fails and returns NULL:
  - title = NULL
  - grub_env_set("grub_menu_title", NULL) - does this set the variable to NULL or does it unset it?
  - grub_free(NULL) - this is safe (free(NULL) is a no-op)- grub_env_export("grub_menu_title") - exports a NULL or unset variable?Then
  when grub_normal_init_page is called:


  - msg_title = grub_env_get("grub_menu_title") - returns NULL if the variable is unset or set to NULL- if (! msg_title) return -
  returns early

  The critical difference is that the old approach would attempt formatting on each call to grub_normal_init_page, whereas the new
  approach only attempts it once during initialization. If that initial formatting fails, subsequent calls will simply exit early
  without retrying, whereas previously there would have been another chance to succeed.

  That said, if memory allocation fails at initialization time, the system is likely already compromised, so the practical impact may
  be minimal. There's also a question about how grub_env_set handles NULL values—whether it unsets the variable or sets it to empty.
  More importantly, after calling grub_env_set with the formatted title string, the code immediately frees that string, which assumes
  grub_env_set creates its own copy of the data rather than storing a pointer to the original allocation. _get("grub_menu_title")
  returns NULL, causing grub_normal_init_page to exit early. Even if the allocation fails, the code handles it gracefully—no title gets
   displayed, but there's no crash or memory leak. The logic appears solid.

∴ Thinking…

  Let me analyze this code carefully. There's a potential bug I need to check.Looking at line 268:```cfor (t = title; *t; t++)
    len += (*t++ == '' ? 4 : 1);

  There's a double increment here! The for loop increments `t` at the end of each iteration (`t++`), and inside the loop body, there's
  another increment (`*t++`).

  So for each iteration:1. Evaluate `*t++` (get current char, then increment t)
  2. Check if it's a single quote
  3. Add 4 or 1 to len4. Loop increment: `t++` (increment t again!)

  This means we're advancing `t` by 2 on each iteration, skipping every other character!

  This would cause the length calculation to be wrong, and might lead to buffer issues.

  Also, the inner increment `*t++` dereferences t and then increments it, but we're not using the result of the dereference in a
  meaningful way - we're comparing the dereferenced value to '\'', but we're already advancing t.

  Let me check the similar function grub_strchrsub to see if it has the same pattern, or if this is intentional.

  Actually, wait. Looking at the code more carefully:```c
  for (t = title; *t; t++)  len += (*t++ == '\'' ? 4 : 1);
  ```The condition is `*t` (dereference without increment). The body is `*t++` (dereference and increment). The loop increment is
  `t++`.

  So:1. Check if `*t` is non-zero (string not ended)
  2. Execute body: `*t++` (get char, compare to '\'', add 4 or 1, then increment t)
  3. Execute loop increment: `t++` (increment t again)

  Yes, this is definitely a double increment bug.


  The fix would be to remove one of the increments—either use `*t` without the post-increment in the body and keep the loop increment,
  or use `*t++` in the body and remove the loop increment. Looking at the grub_strchrsub call on line 327, I need to understand what
  that function does to see if there are related issues elsewhere.

∴ Thinking…

  OK so grub_strchrsub copies input to output, replacing character ch with string with. For a single quote, it replaces it with "'''",
  which has length 4. For other chars, it copies them as-is (length 1).

  So the length calculation in menutitle_prefix should count:- For each '' in the string: 4 bytes
  - For each other character: 1 byte

  But the bug I identified means it's only checking every other character!

  Let me verify this is indeed a bug by looking at the existing setparams_prefix function (which was already in the code) to see if it
  has the same pattern.

● Read(file_path: "/home/lpcs/lpcsf-new/test/rhel/packages/grub2/grub-devel/grub/grub-core/commands/menuentry.c", offset: 224, limit:
      35)
  ⎿  Read 35 lines

∴ Thinking…

  AH! Look at line 236-237:```c
  while (*p)  len += (*p++ == '' ? 4 : 1);

  This is using a while loop with only the increment in the condition expression. There's no loop increment part!So the existing code
  in setparams_prefix is:```c
  while (*p)  len += (*p++ == '\'' ? 4 : 1);```This increments `p` once per iteration (in the body).

  But the new code in menutitle_prefix is:```c
  for (t = title; *t; t++)
    len += (*t++ == '\'' ? 4 : 1);

  This has both a loop increment (t++) AND an increment in the body (*t++), so it increments twice per iteration!

  This is definitely a bug. The correct code should be either:```c
  for (t = title; *t;)  len += (*t++ == '' ? 4 : 1);

  Or:```c
  while (*t)  len += (*t++ == '\'' ? 4 : 1);

  Let me write a review noting this critical bug.

     # AI Review: MR !47 - Option to set title of submenus

     Adds `--menutitle` option to menuentry/submenu commands to customize submenu titles.

     +@command{menuentry}. Nested menus will inherit the menu title, unless
     +explicitly replaces. Using an empty string causes the @var{title} of
     +the option to be used as the menu title.
     +
      All other arguments including @var{title} are passed as positional parameters
      when list of commands is executed with @var{title} always assigned to @code{$1}.
      @end deffn
     @@ -6320,7 +6327,7 @@ when list of commands is executed with @var{title} always assigned to @code{$1}.

      menu; when that entry is selected, a new menu will be displayed showing all
      the entries within this submenu.

∴ Thinking…

  This is replacing the gawk-specific asorti function with piping through sort -u. Let me check if the logic is correct:

  Old code:
  1. Deletes uniqmods array
  2. Adds all dependencies to uniqmods array (which automatically removes duplicates)
  3. Uses asorti to get sorted array
  4. Iterates through sorted array

  New code:
  1. Creates a sort command that writes to mod.deps file
  2. Prints each dependency to the sort command
  3. Closes the sort command (which causes it to run and write to file)
  4. Reads from the mod.deps file

  This looks correct, but let me check for potential issues:
  - The sort_cmd creates a file named after the module with .deps extension
  - sort -u will handle deduplication and sorting
  - The file is read back with getline
  - The file needs to be cleaned up? No, it looks like it's just overwritten each time

  Wait, there's a potential issue: the old code cleared the uniqmods array at the start of each module. The new code doesn't need to do
   this because it creates a new file each time. But is there cleanup of the .deps files? They're probably temporary and cleaned up by
  the build system.

  Let me check if there are any actual bugs:
  1. The sort_cmd is created once per module - correct
  2. Dependencies are piped to sort - correct
  3. sort -u handles deduplication - correct
  4. File is read back - correct
  5. File is implicitly closed after reading - correct

  Actually, I don't see an explicit close for the input file. After the while loop that reads from mod.deps, should there be a close
  statement? Let me check awk behavior - in awk, you generally should close files you opened with getline to avoid file descriptor
  leaks.

● Read(file_path: "/home/lpcs/lpcsf-new/test/rhel/packages/grub2/grub-devel/grub/grub-core/genmoddep.awk", offset: 60, limit: 30)
  ⎿  Read 30 lines

∴ Thinking…

  I see the code. After the while loop that reads from (mod ".deps"), there's no close() statement. This could potentially cause file
  descriptor leaks in awk, especially if there are many modules.

  The pattern is:
  while (getline depmod < (mod ".deps") == 1) {
    ...
  }

  But there's no close(mod ".deps") after this. In awk, you should close files opened with getline to avoid running out of file
  descriptors. This is a minor issue but could cause problems on systems with many modules.

     # AI Review: MR !49 - Replace asorti with portable alternative

     Replaces gawk-specific `asorti` with portable `sort -u` piped to file for deduplication and sorting.

     - **Minor: File descriptor leak** (grub-core/genmoddep.awk:65-70): Opens `mod ".deps"` with getline but never closes it.

     Author: Leo Sandoval <lsandova@redhat.com>
     Date:   Tue Jun 24 13:32:24 2025 -0600

         Create a Containerfiles with required grub packages for development

         Containers bring the ability to have ready-to-use environments and in
         this case complete Fedora and Debian container files are described
         containing all required packages for building and testing grub2.

         Once users manually build it, they can run the desired container, jump
         into a setup ready for development. On the other hand, if users
         prefer to use bare metal instead of a containerized environment, it is
         still useful to have a file explicitly indicating the required packages.

         Signed-off-by: Leo Sandoval <lsandova@redhat.com>

● Bash(git show 2025-06-0183 --stat)
  ⎿  commit 061c47997b69eccca5b6d7670ab4b748d4df1d05
     Author: Khalid Ali <khaliidcaliy@gmail.com>
     Date:   Thu Jun 26 09:23:17 2025 +0000

         kern/efi: Add centralized UEFI error printer

         This RFC patch introduces a UEFI error message printer that translates
         UEFI status codes from hexadecimal values into human-readable strings.

         The problem this rfc solves is, undescriptive error messages in Grub (on failure what
         get printed). Specifically the documented firmware spec which UEFI. In UEFI specific code
         i noticed error messages like "unknown error", "cannot load image" and so on. Unfortunately
         changing error message one by one is expensive and bad idea, all privious attempt resulted
         alot of code duplication. So this idea of centralized error handling for UEFI specific was the
         easiest and cleanest way the problem could be solved.

         The idea is inspired by the GNU C Library's "perror()" function, which translates
         "errno" values into descriptive error messages. Similarly, this implementation eliminates
         the need for conditional statements when printing UEFI error messages. In short, it's a "perror"-like
         mechanism tailored for UEFI status codes in GRUB.

         The function takes three parameter:
           - "grub_err_t err": if the caller desires to set errno. Caller can safely
             return errno if it wants to return status code.
           - "const char *s": a null-terminated string that further
             describes the error context, like the reason of failure.
           - "EFI_STATUS status": the UEFI status code returned by a call to UEFI
             service, to translate to string.

         Additionally, if this patch is accepted, it maybe better if we propogate
         this function throughout UEFI subsystem. That would mean replacing some
         grub_error() calls to this function as it is the one who is calling it
         internally, putting on UEFI service calls in case of failure to check and print
         status codes. Then the caller can safely return "grub_errno" if there
         were a failure. The benefit would be clearer, and more descriptive error messages,
         instead of raw hex codes and reduction of the amount of code needed for
         printing error messages like conditional statements. This will improve user experience,
         debugging clarity and code readability.

         Looking forward for your feedback and what community think of this idea
         and the code. Additionally i left something i felt worth dicussing with
         the commuinty, which is if translation is ideal on this context.

         Best regard
         khaalid

         Signed-off-by: Khalid Ali <khaliidcaliy@gmail.com>

      grub-core/kern/efi/err.c | 81 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
      include/grub/efi/api.h   |  6 ++++
      include/grub/efi/efi.h   |  3 ++
      3 files changed, 90 insertions(+)

∴ Thinking…

  This is adding a new utility function. Let me review the code.

     diff --git a/grub-core/kern/efi/err.c b/grub-core/kern/efi/err.c
     new file mode 100644
     index 000000000..b870b26ba
     --- /dev/null
     +++ b/grub-core/kern/efi/err.c
     @@ -0,0 +1,81 @@
     +/* err.c - uefi specific error handling routine */
     +/*
     + *  GRUB  --  GRand Unified Bootloader
     + *  Copyright (C) 2025 Free Software Foundation, Inc.
     + *
     + *  GRUB is free software: you can redistribute it and/or modify
     + *  it under the terms of the GNU General Public License as published by
     + *  the Free Software Foundation, either version 3 of the License, or
     + *  (at your option) any later version.
     + *
     + *  GRUB is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
     + *  but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
     + *  MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
     + *  GNU General Public License for more details.
     + *
     + *  You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
     + *  along with GRUB.  If not, see <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/>.
     + */
     +
     +#include <grub/err.h>
     +#include <grub/misc.h>
     +#include <grub/efi/api.h>
     +#include <grub/efi/efi.h>
     +
     +struct grub_efi_err
     +{
     +    grub_efi_status_t status;
     +    const char *error_msg;
     +};
     +
     +/* EFI error table: keep entries sorted alphabetically. */
     +static const struct grub_efi_err grub_efi_err_table[] =
     +{
     +    {GRUB_EFI_ABORTED, "aborted"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_ACCESS_DENIED, "access denied"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_ALREADY_STARTED, "already started"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_BAD_BUFFER_SIZE, "bad buffer size"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL, "buffer too small"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_CRC_ERROR, "crc error"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_DEVICE_ERROR, "device error"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_END_OF_FILE, "end of file"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_END_OF_MEDIA, "end of media"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_HTTP_ERROR, "http error"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_ICMP_ERROR, "icmp error"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_INCOMPATIBLE_VERSION, "incompatible version"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_INVALID_PARAMETER, "invalid parameter"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_IP_ADDRESS_CONFLICT, "ip address conflict"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_LOAD_ERROR, "load error"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_MEDIA_CHANGED, "media changed"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_NO_MAPPING, "no mapping"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_NO_MEDIA, "no media"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_NO_RESPONSE, "no response"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_NOT_FOUND, "not found"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_NOT_READY, "not ready"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_NOT_STARTED, "not started"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_OUT_OF_RESOURCES, "out of resources"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_PROTOCOL_ERROR, "protocol error"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_SECURITY_VIOLATION, "security violation"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_SUCCESS, "success"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_TFTP_ERROR, "tftp error"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_TIMEOUT, "timed out"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_UNSPECIFIED_TIMEZONE, "unspecified timezone"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_UNSUPPORTED, "unsupported"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_VOLUME_CORRUPTED, "volume corrupted"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_VOLUME_FULL, "volume full"},
     +    {GRUB_EFI_WRITE_PROTECTED, "write protected"}
     +};
     +
     +void
     +grub_efi_perror(grub_err_t err, const char *s, grub_efi_status_t status)
     +{
     +    for (grub_size_t i = 0; i < ARRAY_SIZE(grub_efi_err_table); i++)
     +    {
     +        if (grub_efi_err_table[i].status == status)
     +        {
     +            grub_error (err, "%s : %s", s, grub_efi_err_table[i].error_msg);
     +            return; /* we got what we needed, that is enough*/
     +        }
     +    }
     +    grub_error (err, "%s : unknown error", s);
     +}
     \ No newline at end of file

∴ Thinking…

  Let me review this code:1. The function takes err, s, and status
  2. It loops through the error table looking for a matching status
  3. If found, it calls grub_error with the error message
  4. If not found, it calls grub_error with "unknown error"Potential issues:
  1. The comment says "keep entries sorted alphabetically" but it's not clear why - is it for binary search? But the code uses linear
  search. This is misleading.2. Missing status codes - the table doesn't include all UEFI error codes. For example, looking at MR !43
  which added GRUB_EFI_COMPROMISED_DATA and GRUB_EFI_INVALID_LANGUAGE, those are not in this table.Actually, wait - this MR might
  predate MR !43. Let me check... MR !43 is 2025-05-0123, MR !51 is 2025-06-0183. So MR !51 comes after and should include those new
  codes!3. GRUB_EFI_UNSPECIFIED_TIMEZONE - I don't see this defined in api.h in other code. Let me check the header changes.

           - "const char *s": a null-terminated string that further
             describes the error context, like the reason of failure.
           - "EFI_STATUS status": the UEFI status code returned by a call to UEFI
             service, to translate to string.

         Additionally, if this patch is accepted, it maybe better if we propogate
         this function throughout UEFI subsystem. That would mean replacing some
         grub_error() calls to this function as it is the one who is calling it
         internally, putting on UEFI service calls in case of failure to check and print
         status codes. Then the caller can safely return "grub_errno" if there
         were a failure. The benefit would be clearer, and more descriptive error messages,
         instead of raw hex codes and reduction of the amount of code needed for
         printing error messages like conditional statements. This will improve user experience,
         debugging clarity and code readability.

         Looking forward for your feedback and what community think of this idea
         and the code. Additionally i left something i felt worth dicussing with
         the commuinty, which is if translation is ideal on this context.

         Best regard
         khaalid

         Signed-off-by: Khalid Ali <khaliidcaliy@gmail.com>

         to tpm, but tpcm adds the function of active monitoring and control to the system.
         It can realize active startup measurement when the system starts,as well as dynamic
         measurement and monitoring when the program is running, further enhance the security

● Read(file_path: "/home/lpcs/lpcsf-new/test/rhel/packages/grub2/grub-devel/grub/grub-core/commands/tpcm.c")
  ⎿  Read 101 lines

∴ Thinking…

  I found a critical bug! Line 68 allocates space with:
  context = grub_zalloc (grub_strlen (str) + grub_strlen (prefix) + 1 + 4);  /* 4 for type */

  But line 72 writes with:
  grub_snprintf (context, 4, "%d|", (type & GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK));

  The issue is that grub_snprintf(context, 4, ...) means "write at most 4 characters INCLUDING the null terminator". So if the type
  number is more than 1 digit, it will be truncated. For example, if type is 10, it would write "10|" + null, but the field "%d|" could
   be "100|" which would need 5 characters.

  But more importantly, the size calculation seems wrong. Let me trace through:
  1. Line 68: allocates strlen(str) + strlen(prefix) + 1 + 4 bytes
  2. Line 72: writes up to 4 bytes for the type (e.g., "0|", "1|", etc.)
  3. Line 74: copies prefix
  4. Line 75: copies str + null terminator

  The problem is the allocation assumes 4 bytes for the type number + delimiter, but grub_snprintf(context, 4, "%d|", ...) limits it to
   4 bytes total including null. If the type is >= 10, it would be "10|" (4 bytes including null), which is correct. But if type >=
  100, it would need 5 bytes.

  Actually, looking more carefully, I think the real issue is that the code assumes the type will fit in 2 digits (allowing for "NN|" +
   null = 4 bytes). But there's no guarantee. The comment says "4 for type" but that's not necessarily enough.

  The snprintf call can only fit types 0-99 before truncation occurs, and there's a type mismatch too—type is enum
  grub_verify_string_type but the code masks it with GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK, which seems semantically wrong.

     # AI Review: MR !52 - Add TPCM module support

     Adds TPCM (Trusted Platform Control Module) verifier for measured boot, similar to TPM but with Chinese national standard.

     - **Missing header file**: grub-core/commands/tpcm.c:24 includes `<grub/efi/tpcm.h>` but file is not added to commit. Causes
     undefined `TPCM_MAX_BUF_SIZE` and `grub_tpcm_measure_memory()`. Won't compile.

     - **Wrong type mask** (grub-core/commands/tpcm.c:72): Uses `(type & GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK)` but `type` is `enum
     grub_verify_string_type`, not `enum grub_file_type`. Masking operation is meaningless.

     - **Buffer overflow risk** (grub-core/commands/tpcm.c:68-75): Allocates `strlen(str) + strlen(prefix) + 5` but
     `grub_snprintf(context, 4, "%d|", type)` truncates type numbers >= 100 to 3 digits. If verify string types can be >= 100, this
      causes silent data corruption.

● Bash(git show 7b037605ecbcdb4649fcb31fd8265e4169365518)
  ⎿  commit 7b037605ecbcdb4649fcb31fd8265e4169365518
     Author: Egor Ignatov <egori@altlinux.org>
     Date:   Thu Mar 19 16:57:04 2026 +0500

         commands/bli: Do not treat non-GPT partitions as an error

         Currently, get_part_uuid() returns GRUB_ERR_BAD_PART_TABLE when the
         boot device uses a non-GPT partition table (e.g., MBR). This causes
         set_loader_device_part_uuid() to report a spurious error message to the
         user, even though the boot continues normally.

         As documented, the bli module ignores non-GPT partitions and simply
         does not set LoaderDevicePartUUID in this case. Align the code with
         this behavior.

         Fix get_part_uuid() to return GRUB_ERR_NONE with part_uuid set to NULL
         when the partition is not GPT, and adjust set_loader_device_part_uuid()
         to only report an error when get_part_uuid() actually fails.

         Signed-off-by: Egor Ignatov <egori@altlinux.org>

     diff --git a/grub-core/commands/bli.c b/grub-core/commands/bli.c
     index 38f52f87a..dfae671b8 100644
     --- a/grub-core/commands/bli.c
     +++ b/grub-core/commands/bli.c
     @@ -75,9 +75,11 @@ get_part_uuid (const char *device_name, char **part_uuid)

        if (grub_strcmp (device->disk->partition->partmap->name, "gpt") != 0)
          {
     -      status = grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_PART_TABLE,
     -			   N_("this is not a GPT partition table: %s"), device_name);
     -      goto fail;
     +      grub_dprintf ("bli", "%s is not a GPT partition, partuuid skipped\n", device_name);
     +      *part_uuid = NULL;
     +      grub_disk_close (disk);
     +      grub_device_close (device);
     +      return GRUB_ERR_NONE;
          }

        if (grub_disk_read (disk, device->disk->partition->offset,
     @@ -120,7 +122,7 @@ set_loader_device_part_uuid (void)
          status = grub_efi_set_variable_to_string ("LoaderDevicePartUUID", &bli_vendor_guid, part_uuid,
      					      GRUB_EFI_VARIABLE_BOOTSERVICE_ACCESS |
      					      GRUB_EFI_VARIABLE_RUNTIME_ACCESS);
     -  else
     +  else if (status != GRUB_ERR_NONE)
          grub_error (status, N_("unable to determine partition UUID of boot device"));

        grub_free (part_uuid);

● Bash(git show 129588e498647842c6f13f7809ef9ac583e2351b)
  ⎿  commit 129588e498647842c6f13f7809ef9ac583e2351b
     Author: Egor Ignatov <egori@altlinux.org>
     Date:   Thu Mar 19 17:22:11 2026 +0500

         util/grub.d/00_header.in: Load efi_uga only on x86 EFI platforms

         The efi_uga module is only built for i386-efi and x86_64-efi firmware), but
         commit ea0b76dc4a ("Disable loading all_video for EFI") loads it unconditionally
         on all EFI platforms. This causes a "file not found" error on aarch64-efi and
         other non-x86 EFI targets.

         Guard the insmod with a $grub_cpu check so the module is only
         loaded where it is actually available.

         Fixes: ea0b76dc4 ("util/grub.d/00_header.in: Disable loading all_video for EFI")

         Signed-off-by: Egor Ignatov <egori@altlinux.org>

     diff --git a/util/grub.d/00_header.in b/util/grub.d/00_header.in

     +++ b/util/grub.d/00_header.in
     @@ -135,7 +135,9 @@ else # GRUB_FORCE_EFI_ALL_VIDEO is not set true
          cat <<EOF
        if [ x\$grub_platform = xefi ]; then
          insmod efi_gop
     -    insmod efi_uga
     +    if [ x\$grub_cpu = xi386 -o x\$grub_cpu = xx86_64 ]; then
     +      insmod efi_uga
     +    fi
        elif [ x\$feature_all_video_module = xy ]; then
      EOF
      fi # end GRUB_FORCE_EFI_ALL_VIDEO

     +
     +grub_size_t
     +grub_list_length (grub_list_t head)
     +{
     +  grub_list_t item;
     +  grub_size_t length = 0;
     +
     +  FOR_LIST_ELEMENTS (item, head)
     +    length++;
     +
     +  return length;
     +}
     diff --git a/include/grub/list.h b/include/grub/list.h
     index 21f4b4b44..094eaf9cd 100644

      {
     @@ -33,6 +34,7 @@ typedef struct grub_list *grub_list_t;

      void EXPORT_FUNC(grub_list_push) (grub_list_t *head, grub_list_t item);
      void EXPORT_FUNC(grub_list_remove) (grub_list_t item);
     +grub_size_t EXPORT_FUNC(grub_list_length) (grub_list_t head);

      #define FOR_LIST_ELEMENTS(var, list) for ((var) = (list); (var); (var) = (var)->next)
      #define FOR_LIST_ELEMENTS_NEXT(var, list) for ((var) = (var)->next; (var); (var) = (var)->next)

● Read(file_path: "/home/lpcs/lpcsf-new/test/rhel/packages/grub2/grub-devel/grub/grub-core/commands/tpcm.c", offset: 68, limit: 10)
  ⎿  Read 10 lines

∴ Thinking…

  Line 68: allocates strlen(str) + strlen(prefix) + 1 + 4Line 72: grub_snprintf(context, 4, "%d|", ...) - this has a maximum size of 4,
   which means it can write at most 3 characters + null terminator. If type is >= 100, it would be truncated to 2 digits + '|' + null =
   4 bytes total.Wait, actually let me reconsider. The grub_snprintf signature is grub_snprintf(char *str, grub_size_t n, const char
  *fmt, ...). The second parameter n is the size of the buffer. So grub_snprintf(context, 4, "%d|", type) means:- Write at most 4 bytes
   to context- Format: "%d|" which is a decimal number + pipe characterIf type is 0-9: "0|", "1|", etc. = 2 chars + null = 3 bytesIf
  type is 10-99: "10|", "99|", etc. = 3 chars + null = 4 bytesIf type is 100-999: would need 4 chars + null = 5 bytes, but only 4 bytes
   available, so truncatedMy review said "type numbers >= 100 truncates to 3 digits". Actually, with size 4, it would truncate the pipe
   character, not the third digit. Let me re-check this logic.Actually, wait. The buffer allocated is strlen(str) + strlen(prefix) + 1
  + 4. So there are 4 bytes allocated specifically for the type. But then grub_snprintf(context, 4, ...) says "write at most 4 bytes".
  So this should be fine...Actually no! I think I misunderstood. Let me re-read the code. Line 68 allocates the total buffer. Line 72
  writes to the beginning of that buffer with size limit 4.

  After line 72 completes, description = context + grub_strlen(context) moves the pointer to where the type string ends. Then the
  prefix and str are appended after that. The real issue emerges when type values exceed 99—the snprintf call with a 4-byte limit would
   drop the pipe delimiter if the number itself takes 3 digits, leaving the format malformed.

  However, checking the actual enum values for grub_verify_string_type reveals they're just 0, 1, and 2, so overflow isn't actually a
  concern there. The real problem is the mask being applied—using (type & GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK) instead of just type changes what values
   get written. I need to understand what GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK actually evaluates to in order to determine if this creates a real issue.

  ⎿  20933e9de efi/tpcm: Add complete support of TPCM module

∴ Thinking…

  Excellent! So the commit only adds:
  - grub-core/Makefile.core.def
  - grub-core/commands/tpcm.c

  Now, I saw from the system reminder that tpcm.c includes:
  #include <grub/efi/tpcm.h>

  But the file include/grub/efi/tpcm.h is NOT in the commit. So my first claim is CORRECT - missing header file.

  Now let me verify the other claims. I need to look at the actual code. I already have tpcm.c in the system reminders above. Let me
  check:

  Line 24: #include <grub/efi/tpcm.h> - ✓ Correct, and this file is not in commit

  Line 28: static char context_buf[TPCM_MAX_BUF_SIZE]; - This uses TPCM_MAX_BUF_SIZE which would be undefined without the header - ✓
  Correct

  Line 45: return grub_tpcm_measure_memory (context, (grub_addr_t)buf, size); - Uses grub_tpcm_measure_memory which would be undefined
  without the header - ✓ Correct

  Now for the second claim about wrong type mask at line 72:
  grub_snprintf (context, 4, "%d|", (type & GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK));

  The parameter is enum grub_verify_string_type type (line 49). Let me check what GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK is and whether it makes sense
  here.

  Actually wait - let me look more carefully. Line 35 also has:
  grub_snprintf (context_buf, TPCM_MAX_BUF_SIZE, "%d|%s", (type & GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK), io->name);



  So they're using GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK on the type parameter. But in line 31, the function signature is:
  grub_tpcm_verify_init (grub_file_t io, enum grub_file_type type, void **context,
                         enum grub_verify_flags *flags)

  So in grub_tpcm_verify_init, the type parameter IS enum grub_file_type - so using GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK is correct there! But in
  grub_tpcm_verify_string, the type is enum grub_verify_string_type, which is a different enum entirely. Using GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK on a
   different enum type is semantically wrong - the mask is designed for file type enums, not string verification types.

  Now looking at the buffer overflow issue in lines 68-75, the allocation is grub_strlen(str) + grub_strlen(prefix) + 1 + 4 bytes, but
  then grub_snprintf is called with a buffer size of only 4 bytes to write the type and pipe character. This will overflow the buffer
  since the formatted output will be much larger than 4 bytes.


  c
  description = context + grub_strlen (context);
  grub_memcpy (description, prefix, grub_strlen (prefix));
  grub_memcpy (description + grub_strlen (prefix), str, grub_strlen (str) + 1);

  The subsequent memory operations then copy the prefix and string into the buffer after the type field, but the initial snprintf call
  has already corrupted the memory layout by writing beyond its 4-byte boundary. writing into context with size 4, which is just for
  the type prefix. The total buffer is much larger since it includes space for the string and prefix too.

  Looking at this more carefully: the allocation is strlen(str) + strlen(prefix) + 1 + 4 bytes total, but the first snprintf call only
  uses 4 bytes of that space. If the type is 100 or higher (three digits), snprintf needs 5 bytes for "100|" plus null terminator, but
  it's constrained to 4 bytes, so it truncates to "100\0" and loses the pipe character. Then description gets set to point into this
  buffer. digits without a separator, then 4 bytes isn't always enough. If type is 100-999, that's 3 digits, plus we need the "|"
  separator, so that's 4 bytes minimum. But the code only allocates 4 bytes total for the type prefix, which works for single/double
  digit types with "|", but fails when type is triple digits because the "|" gets dropped.

  The real problem is the format string doesn't handle larger type values correctly—it assumes the type prefix will always fit in 4
  bytes, but three-digit types need that space for the digits themselves, leaving no room for the separator. + 1 = strlen(prefix) +
  strlen(str) + 6

  So the allocation is strlen(str) + strlen(prefix) + 5 bytes, but I need strlen(prefix) + strlen(str) + 6 for the 3-digit case. That's
   a 1-byte overflow. Let me trace through the actual code path: the snprintf call writes to this buffer with a format that includes
  the type prefix, so I need to verify the exact format string and how many bytes it actually consumes.

  The snprintf writes the type value followed by a pipe character, but it's bounded by the buffer size passed to it, so it will
  truncate rather than overflow. After that, the description pointer is set to wherever the string ends, and then the prefix and
  original string are copied in. The final layout would have the type digits, followed by the prefix, then the original string with its
   null terminator.

  Looking at the space available: the buffer was allocated with enough room for the original string, prefix, and some extra bytes.
  After the snprintf occupies the first few bytes, the remaining space should accommodate both the prefix and string being copied. The
  calculation shows there's exactly enough room—the available space after the initial write matches what's needed for the subsequent
  memcpy operations, so no overflow occurs.

  However, there's a subtle issue with how the null terminators interact. The snprintf places a null at offset 3, but then the prefix
  gets written directly over that position, effectively removing the null terminator from the first part of the buffer. When the string
   is then copied after the prefix, the final memcpy includes its own null terminator, which ends up being the only null in the buffer.

  The real problem isn't a buffer overflow—the math works out to 11 bytes used from 12 allocated. The issue is that the format string
  is malformed: it should produce "100|cmd: ls" but instead produces "100cmd: ls" because the pipe character from the snprintf format
  string gets lost when the prefix overwrites that position.

  My review labeled this as "Buffer overflow risk" but described it as "silent data corruption," which is more accurate. The actual
  concern is whether enum grub_verify_string_type can have values >= 100, which would cause the snprintf to truncate and corrupt the
  output format. I need to check the enum definition to confirm if this is a real vulnerability or a theoretical one.

  Looking at the code, the enum values in the switch statement (GRUB_VERIFY_KERNEL_CMDLINE, GRUB_VERIFY_MODULE_CMDLINE,
  GRUB_VERIFY_COMMAND) appear to be small single or double-digit values, so in practice the buffer would handle them fine. However,
  there's still a conceptual flaw in how the code handles type formatting.

  The allocation of 4 bytes for the type assumes it will be at most 3 digits plus a null terminator, but if any type value reaches 100
  or higher, snprintf would truncate the output and lose the pipe delimiter. This creates a silent data corruption issue rather than a
  true buffer overflow. The concern is valid for types >= 100, though unlikely given the current enum definitions. The "format
  corruption risk" might be a more precise characterization than "buffer overflow," but the underlying issue remains—if type values
  ever grow beyond two digits, the string formatting breaks silently.

  Additionally, there's a semantic problem: the code uses GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK on an enum grub_verify_string_type parameter, when that
  mask is designed for enum grub_file_type. This type mismatch is a separate correctness issue independent of the buffer sizing
  concern.

  Looking back at the buffer calculation, I need to trace through what's actually being written. The allocation includes
  grub_strlen(str) + grub_strlen(prefix) + 1 + 4, where the "+4" accounts for the snprintf output and the "+1" appears to be padding.
  The final string format is "N|", so I should verify whether the math actually covers all components being concatenated.

  The type prefix "N|" varies in length depending on the number—it could be 2 characters for single digits, 3 for double digits, or 4
  for triple digits. Since they're allocating a fixed 4 bytes for this portion, there's enough buffer for any reasonable type value,
  though it's slightly wasteful for smaller numbers.

  However, the snprintf call limits output to exactly 4 bytes, which creates a problem when the type exceeds 999. For a type like 100,
  snprintf writes "100|" plus a null terminator into that 4-byte space, truncating the result to "100\0". This means the description
  pointer ends up at offset 3 instead of where it should be, and the subsequent writes of prefix and str begin at the wrong position in
   the buffer, corrupting the layout.

  Looking at the actual space available: we allocate strlen(str) + strlen(prefix) + 5 bytes total, but need strlen(prefix) +
  strlen(str) + 4 bytes (3 for "100", plus the prefix, str, and null terminator). So there's technically 1 extra byte—no overflow
  occurs. The real issue is the format itself becomes malformed: instead of "100|", we get "100" because the pipe character gets
  truncated away.

  The review's title mentions "buffer overflow" but that's imprecise—the actual problem is silent data corruption from format
  truncation. The description itself is accurate about the corruption risk, so while the title could be clearer, it's not fundamentally
   wrong.

  Looking more carefully though, the practical risk seems minimal. Enum values stay small due to GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK, and even if a
  value exceeded 99 digits, the consequence would just be a missing delimiter character rather than a genuine security vulnerability.
  The issue is technically valid as a bug, but framing it as a "risk" might overstate its severity.

  The core problem actually traces back to item #2—using the wrong type mask. If the correct mask were applied, type values would
  remain in the 0-2 range from the switch statement, eliminating any truncation concern entirely. So the truncation issue is really a
  secondary consequence of the primary mask error.

  I should refocus the review: keep the critical missing header issue and the clear type mask bug, but drop the buffer overflow claim
  since it's dependent on the mask problem and overstates the actual risk.

∴ Thinking…

  Good! So the commit only adds:
  - grub-core/Makefile.core.def
  - grub-core/commands/tpcm.c

  And from the system reminder, I can see that tpcm.c line 24 includes:
  #include <grub/efi/tpcm.h>

  But include/grub/efi/tpcm.h is NOT in the commit. So claim #1 (missing header) is CORRECT.

  Now let me verify claim #2 (wrong type mask at line 72). From the code in the system reminder:

  Line 49: grub_tpcm_verify_string (char *str, enum grub_verify_string_type type)
  Line 72: grub_snprintf (context, 4, "%d|", (type & GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK));

  So the parameter is enum grub_verify_string_type type, but the code uses GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK. This is wrong - the mask is for enum
  grub_file_type, not enum grub_verify_string_type. Claim #2 is CORRECT.

  Now for claim #3 (buffer overflow risk). Let me analyze this more carefully:

  Line 68: context = grub_zalloc (grub_strlen (str) + grub_strlen (prefix) + 1 + 4);  /* 4 for type */
  Line 72: grub_snprintf (context, 4, "%d|", (type & GRUB_FILE_TYPE_MASK));

  The allocation is: strlen(str) + strlen(prefix) + 1 + 4 = strlen(str) + strlen(prefix) + 5

  After snprintf, if type is 100, it would try to write "100|" (4 chars + null = 5 bytes) but the buffer size parameter is 4, so it
  truncates to "100\0" (missing the "|").

  Then at line 73: description = context + grub_strlen (context); sets description to point after the type string. If type was 100,
  that's 3 bytes in, so description points to offset 3. The code then writes prefix and str at that location. The total space needed is
   3 + strlen(prefix) + strlen(str) + 1, but only 5 bytes were allocated total, leaving just 2 bytes of buffer after the type portion.
  This is a real overflow risk since prefix and str could easily exceed that remaining space.

  Actually, wait - there's 1 extra byte available, so no overflow occurs. But the real problem is the format gets corrupted: it
  produces "100" instead of "100|" (the pipe separator is missing). In practice though, the enum values are small integers (0-2 range
  for GRUB_VERIFY_KERNEL_CMDLINE and similar), so type would never reach 100. The underlying issue is claim #2 about the incorrect mask
   - if the right type values were used directly, this format corruption wouldn't manifest. So claim #3 overstates the risk; it's not
  truly a buffer overflow vulnerability, and the data corruption only happens with unrealistic type values.

  I should drop claim #3 from the review since it depends on claim #2 and mischaracterizes the actual problem.

● Bash(git show 7b037605e --format=fuller)
  ⎿  commit 7b037605ecbcdb4649fcb31fd8265e4169365518
     Author:     Egor Ignatov <egori@altlinux.org>
     AuthorDate: Thu Mar 19 16:57:04 2026 +0500
     Commit:     Leo Sandoval <lsandova@redhat.com>
     CommitDate: Thu Mar 19 15:17:53 2026 -0600

         commands/bli: Do not treat non-GPT partitions as an error

         Currently, get_part_uuid() returns GRUB_ERR_BAD_PART_TABLE when the
         boot device uses a non-GPT partition table (e.g., MBR). This causes
         set_loader_device_part_uuid() to report a spurious error message to the
         user, even though the boot continues normally.

         As documented, the bli module ignores non-GPT partitions and simply
         does not set LoaderDevicePartUUID in this case. Align the code with
         this behavior.

         Fix get_part_uuid() to return GRUB_ERR_NONE with part_uuid set to NULL
         when the partition is not GPT, and adjust set_loader_device_part_uuid()
         to only report an error when get_part_uuid() actually fails.

         Signed-off-by: Egor Ignatov <egori@altlinux.org>

     diff --git a/grub-core/commands/bli.c b/grub-core/commands/bli.c
     index 38f52f87a..dfae671b8 100644

     +++ b/grub-core/commands/bli.c
     @@ -75,9 +75,11 @@ get_part_uuid (const char *device_name, char **part_uuid)

          {
     -      status = grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_PART_TABLE,
     -			   N_("this is not a GPT partition table: %s"), device_name);
     -      goto fail;
     +      grub_dprintf ("bli", "%s is not a GPT partition, partuuid skipped\n", device_name);
     +      *part_uuid = NULL;
     +      grub_disk_close (disk);
     +      grub_device_close (device);
     +      return GRUB_ERR_NONE;
          }

        if (grub_disk_read (disk, device->disk->partition->offset,
     @@ -120,7 +122,7 @@ set_loader_device_part_uuid (void)

      					      GRUB_EFI_VARIABLE_BOOTSERVICE_ACCESS |
      					      GRUB_EFI_VARIABLE_RUNTIME_ACCESS);
     -  else
     +  else if (status != GRUB_ERR_NONE)
          grub_error (status, N_("unable to determine partition UUID of boot device"));

        grub_free (part_uuid);

     +{
     +  grub_list_t item;
     +  grub_size_t length = 0;
     +
     +  FOR_LIST_ELEMENTS (item, head)
     +    length++;
     +
     +  return length;
     +}

     +grub_size_t EXPORT_FUNC(grub_list_length) (grub_list_t head);

      #define FOR_LIST_ELEMENTS(var, list) for ((var) = (list); (var); (var) = (var)->next)
      #define FOR_LIST_ELEMENTS_NEXT(var, list) for ((var) = (var)->next; (var); (var) = (var)->next)

        libgcrypt-grub: implement _gcry_get_hw_features()

  This commit adds a patch file for libgcrypt. The patch implements _gcry_get_hw_features() which detects hardware features. Let me
  analyze the code:Lines 1-10: Standard includes for libgcrypt
  Lines 12-28: Implementation of _gcry_get_hw_features()The function:
  1. Uses static variables to cache the detection result
  2. Returns cached value if already detected
  3. For x86_64, calls _gcry_hwf_detect_x86()
  4. Sets detected = true
  5. Returns hw_featuresIssues I should look for:
  6. Is the caching logic thread-safe? (No locks, but GRUB is likely single-threaded)
  7. Is the x86_64 check correct?
  8. Are there any logic errors?Let me think about potential issues:1. Missing initialization: hw_features is declared but not
  explicitly initialized. In C, static variables are zero-initialized, so this is OK.2. Limited platform support: Only x86_64 is
  supported. For other platforms, hw_features will be 0. This seems intentional.
  9. Caching without synchronization: The detected flag is checked before hw_features is set. In theory, if this were multi-threaded,
  there could be a race condition. But GRUB is single-threaded, so this is OK.4. Typo in commit message: "libgcryt" should be
  "libgcrypt" - but that's in the commit message, not the code, so not a functional issue.

  Actually, wait. Let me look more carefully. The patch file shows it's creating a new file hwfeatures.c with this code. But I need to
  check if the patch is correctly formatted and if _gcry_hwf_detect_x86() is defined elsewhere.

  non-x86_64 platforms, the function returns 0 from the static variable, indicating no hardware features are available, which is the

● Bash(git log --oneline da7dd6ef0^..da7dd6ef0)
  ⎿  da7dd6ef0 libgcrypt-grub: implement _gcry_get_hw_features()

∴ Thinking…

  Just one commit. Let me review the code more carefully for any issues:

  unsigned int
  _gcry_get_hw_features(void)
  {
    static bool detected;
    static unsigned int hw_features;

    if (detected == true)
      return hw_features;

  #if defined(__x86_64__)
    hw_features = _gcry_hwf_detect_x86();
  #endif

    detected = true;

    return hw_features;
  }

  Potential issues:1. Comparison style: if (detected == true) could be if (detected) but this is a style issue, not a bug.

  2. Race condition in caching: Lines 18-24 have a classic TOCTOU (time-of-check-time-of-use) pattern:
  if (detected == true)   // Check
    return hw_features;
  // ... time passes ...
  hw_features = ...;      // Use
  detected = true;

  2. But in a multi-threaded environment, if two threads call this simultaneously:
    - Thread A checks detected, sees false
    - Thread B checks detected, sees false
    - Thread A sets hw_features and detected = true
    - Thread B sets hw_features and detected = true

  Both threads run the detection code, which is wasteful but not harmful (they'll get the same result).

  However, there's a worse scenario:
    - Thread A checks detected, sees false
    - Thread A starts executing hw_features = _gcry_hwf_detect_x86()
    - Thread A is interrupted before setting detected = true
    - Thread B checks detected, sees false
    - Thread B executes hw_features = _gcry_hwf_detect_x86()
    - Thread B sets detected = true
    - Thread B returns while Thread A is still running the detection function. When Thread A finishes, it overwrites detected and
  hw_features, potentially causing the detection to run multiple times or leaving inconsistent state if the function has side effects.

  However, GRUB operates single-threaded, so concurrent access isn't actually a concern here. The patch also pulls in headers like
  <unistd.h> that may not exist in GRUB's build environment, though since this is in the libgcrypt-patches directory, it's likely
  intended for the libgcrypt compilation rather than GRUB itself.

  Looking at the patch path more carefully—grub-core/lib/libgcrypt-patches/08_add_hwfeatures.patch—this is being applied to libgcrypt
  during the GRUB build, so the standard library includes should work fine in that context. The patch creates a new file
  grub-core/lib/libgcrypt-grub/src/hwfeatures.c that detects CPU hardware capabilities and caches the result to avoid repeated
  detection overhead. ` (built-in)
  - _gcry_hwf_detect_x86() (from hwf-common.h)

  Most of these includes aren't actually needed by the code. The logic itself appears sound—caching works fine for GRUB's
  single-threaded environment, static variables initialize correctly, and the x86-64 platform check is properly guarded. There aren't
  any functional issues I can identify, just some unnecessary header includes that don't affect the implementation.

● Reviewing the code - this adds hardware feature detection for libgcrypt. The             11:46 PM  claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929
  implementation looks straightforward with caching. Let me check for any issues:

  The code caches detection results and calls platform-specific detection. No issues found
   - the logic is correct, static variables are properly initialized, and platform guards
  are appropriate. GRUB is single-threaded so no synchronization needed.

● Bash(git show e2f2470dd --stat)
  ⎿  commit e2f2470ddc8d2ee89dc0f40f504dc89752ef61c1
     Author: Khalid Ali <khaliidcaly@gmail.com>
     Date:   Mon Jul 21 07:55:44 2025 +0000

         commands/efi: Add a command to dump all UEFI runtime variables

         This command is intended to print or dump all UEFI runtime services.
         The structure will look like efivar tool, since visually most people are
         familiar with it. All uefi variables are dumped as hex no matter what
         data type is.

         Signed-off-by: Khalid Ali <khaliidcaly@gmail.com>

● Bash(git show e2f2470dd grub-core/commands/efi/lsefivar.c)
  ⎿  commit e2f2470ddc8d2ee89dc0f40f504dc89752ef61c1
     Author: Khalid Ali <khaliidcaly@gmail.com>
     Date:   Mon Jul 21 07:55:44 2025 +0000

         commands/efi: Add a command to dump all UEFI runtime variables

         This command is intended to print or dump all UEFI runtime services.
         The structure will look like efivar tool, since visually most people are
         familiar with it. All uefi variables are dumped as hex no matter what
         data type is.

         Signed-off-by: Khalid Ali <khaliidcaly@gmail.com>

     diff --git a/grub-core/commands/efi/lsefivar.c b/grub-core/commands/efi/lsefivar.c
     new file mode 100644
     index 000000000..8bbafdc44
     --- /dev/null
     +++ b/grub-core/commands/efi/lsefivar.c
     @@ -0,0 +1,137 @@
     +/* efivar.c - dump runtime uefi variables. */
     +/*
     + *  GRUB  --  GRand Unified Bootloader
     + *  Copyright (C) 2025  Free Software Foundation, Inc.
     + *
     + *  GRUB is free software: you can redistribute it and/or modify
     + *  it under the terms of the GNU General Public License as published by
     + *  the Free Software Foundation, either version 3 of the License, or
     + *  (at your option) any later version.
     + *
     + *  GRUB is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
     + *  but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
     + *  MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
     + *  GNU General Public License for more details.
     + *
     + *  You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
     + *  along with GRUB.  If not, see <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/>.
     + */
     +
     +
     +
     +#include <grub/dl.h>
     +#include <grub/misc.h>
     +#include <grub/charset.h>
     +#include <grub/extcmd.h>
     +#include <grub/efi/efi.h>
     +#include <grub/efi/api.h>
     +#include <grub/lib/hexdump.h>
     +
     +GRUB_MOD_LICENSE ("GPLv3+");
     +
     +static const struct grub_arg_option options[] =
     +{
     +       {0, 'l', 0, N_("Dump variable contents"), 0, 0},
     +       {0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0}
     +};
     +
     +static void
     +dump_variable_data(const char *variable_name, grub_guid_t *variable_guid)
     +{
     +    grub_efi_status_t status;
     +    grub_efi_uint32_t attributes;
     +    grub_size_t data_size;
     +    void *data;
     +
     +    status = grub_efi_get_variable_with_attributes (variable_name, variable_guid,
     +                                                   &data_size, &data, &attributes);
     +    if (status != GRUB_EFI_SUCCESS)
     +    {
     +        grub_errno = GRUB_ERR_NONE;
     +        return;
     +    }
     +
     +    grub_puts_ (N_("Attributes:"));
     +    if (attributes & GRUB_EFI_VARIABLE_NON_VOLATILE)
     +        grub_puts_ (N_("\tNon-Volatile"));
     +    if (attributes & GRUB_EFI_VARIABLE_BOOTSERVICE_ACCESS)
     +        grub_puts_ (N_("\tBoot Service Access"));
     +    if (attributes & GRUB_EFI_VARIABLE_RUNTIME_ACCESS)
     +        grub_puts_ (N_("\tRuntime Service Access"));
     +
     +    grub_puts_ (N_("Value:"));
     +    hexdump (0, data, data_size);
     +    grub_free (data);
     +}
     +
     +static grub_err_t
     +dump_efi_variables(grub_efi_boolean_t dump_variable_content)
     +{
     +       grub_efi_status_t status;
     +       grub_efi_runtime_services_t *r;
     +       grub_efi_uintn_t variable_name_size = 512;
     +       grub_efi_char16_t *variable_name;
     +       grub_uint8_t *variable_name_string = NULL;
     +       grub_guid_t vendor_guid;
     +
     +       r = grub_efi_system_table->runtime_services;
     +
     +       variable_name = grub_calloc (variable_name_size, sizeof(grub_efi_char16_t));
     +       if (variable_name == NULL)
     +               return grub_errno;
     +       *variable_name = 0; /* Start with empty string */
     +
     +       while (1)
     +       {
     +               status = r->get_next_variable_name (&variable_name_size, variable_name, &vendor_guid);
     +               if (status == GRUB_EFI_NOT_FOUND)
     +                       break;
     +               else if (status == GRUB_EFI_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL)
     +               {
     +                       grub_efi_char16_t *tmp;
     +                       grub_dprintf ("lsefivar", "buffer isn't enough, enlarging up to %ld bytes\n",variable_name_size *
     sizeof(grub_efi_char16_t));
     +                       tmp = grub_realloc (variable_name, variable_name_size * sizeof(grub_efi_char16_t));
     +                       if (tmp == NULL)
     +                       {
     +                               grub_free (variable_name);
     +                               return grub_errno;
     +                       }
     +                       variable_name = tmp;
     +               }
     +               variable_name_string = grub_calloc (variable_name_size + 1, GRUB_MAX_UTF8_PER_UTF16);
     +               if (variable_name_string == NULL)
     +               {
     +                       grub_free (variable_name);
     +                       return grub_errno;
     +               }
     +               *grub_utf16_to_utf8 (variable_name_string, variable_name, variable_name_size) = '\0';
     +               grub_printf ("%pG-%s\n",&vendor_guid, variable_name_string);
     +               if (dump_variable_content)
     +                       dump_variable_data ((char*)variable_name_string, &vendor_guid);
     +       }
     +
     +       grub_free (variable_name);
     +       grub_free (variable_name_string);
     +       return GRUB_ERR_NONE;
     +}
     +
     +static grub_err_t
     +grub_cmd_lsefivar (grub_extcmd_context_t ctxt,
     +                int argc __attribute__((unused)), char **argv __attribute__((unused)))
     +{
     +       if (ctxt->state[0].set)
     +               return dump_efi_variables (true); /* true: dump variables and contents*/
     +
     +       return dump_efi_variables (false); /* false: do not dump variable contents*/
     +}
     +
     +static grub_extcmd_t cmd;
     +
     +GRUB_MOD_INIT(lsefivar)
     +{
     +       cmd = grub_register_extcmd ("lsefivar", grub_cmd_lsefivar, 0, N_("[-l]"), N_("Display UEFI variables."), options);
     +}
     +GRUB_MOD_FINI(lsefivar)
     +{
     +       grub_unregister_extcmd (cmd);
     +}

∴ Thinking…

  Let me analyze this code for issues:1. Line 45-46: Uses grub_efi_get_variable_with_attributes but passes variable_name as a const
  char* when it should be grub_efi_char16_t* (wide characters). Wait, let me check the function signature. Actually, looking at line
  108, they pass variable_name_string which is UTF-8. So line 109 should pass the wide-char variable_name, not variable_name_string.
  Actually, on second look, line 40 shows dump_variable_data takes const char *variable_name, and line 109 calls it with
  (char*)variable_name_string which is UTF-8. Then line 45 calls grub_efi_get_variable_with_attributes with variable_name. This seems
  backwards - the EFI function expects wide chars, but they're passing a UTF-8 string.   Actually, wait. Line 40 declares const char
  *variable_name, and line 45 uses it. But looking at the caller (line 109), they pass (char*)variable_name_string which is UTF-8. But
  the EFI function expects wide chars.

     This is a bug: dump_variable_data should take grub_efi_char16_t *variable_name not const char *variable_name, and line 109 should
  pass variable_name not variable_name_string.

  2. Line 91: Debug message has a potential format issue - %ld for variable_name_size * sizeof(grub_efi_char16_t) but
  variable_name_size is grub_efi_uintn_t which might not be long. Should use %lu or %zu.3. Line 100: Allocates variable_name_string
  inside the loop but only frees it at line 113 after the loop.

  The pointer gets overwritten on each iteration without freeing the previous allocation, creating a memory leak where only the final
  allocation is freed.

  4. Line 106: grub_utf16_to_utf8 returns a pointer to the byte after the last written byte, and they dereference it to write '\0'.
  This is correct.
  5. Line 85: After get_next_variable_name returns with GRUB_EFI_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL, they reallocate and continue, but they don't retry
  the call itself - the function needs to be invoked again with the larger buffer to actually retrieve the variable name.

  Looking at the control flow more carefully, after the realloc block at line 88-99, there's no continue statement, so execution falls
  through to lines 100-110 where it tries to process the variable. But get_next_variable_name failed and returned an error, so this is
  a bug - the code should skip to the next iteration with continue; after the realloc, not attempt to process invalid data.

  Additionally, there's an unhandled case: if get_next_variable_name returns any status other than GRUB_EFI_NOT_FOUND or
  GRUB_EFI_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL, the code doesn't explicitly handle it and still proceeds to process the variable name, which could be
  problematic.

  I should also verify the type mismatch on line 45 where variable_name is declared as const char * but gets passed to
  grub_efi_get_variable_with_attributes, which likely expects a wide-character string instead.

  The memory leak from variable_name_string not being freed in the loop is definitely problematic, as is the missing continue statement
   after the realloc call and the absent success check after processing the variable.

● Looking at this code, I see several issues:                                              11:50 PM  claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929

● Write(/home/lpcs/lpcsf-new/test/rhel/packages/grub2/grub-devel/reviews/2025-07-0196.md)
  ⎿  Wrote 10 lines to /home/lpcs/lpcsf-new/test/rhel/packages/grub2/grub-devel/reviews/2025-07-0196.md
     # AI Review: MR !59 - Add lsefivar command to dump UEFI variables

     Adds command to list/dump UEFI runtime variables. Several bugs found:

     - **Memory leak** (line 100): `variable_name_string` allocated in loop but only freed once at line 113. Each iteration leaks
     the previous allocation. Should `grub_free(variable_name_string)` at end of each loop iteration.

     - **Missing continue after realloc** (line 88-99): When `GRUB_EFI_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL`, code reallocates buffer but falls through
      to process invalid data. Should add `continue;` after line 99 to retry `get_next_variable_name()` with larger buffer.

     - **Missing error handling** (line 85): If `get_next_variable_name()` returns error other than `NOT_FOUND` or
     `BUFFER_TOO_SMALL`, code still processes invalid data. Should check `status == GRUB_EFI_SUCCESS` or handle other errors.

        From 1e062220d64ce1cf491ad670f368c06072668d2b Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
        From: Shreenidhi Shedi <shreenidhi.shedi@broadcom.com>
        Date: Wed, 14 May 2025 14:47:15 +0530
        Subject: [PATCH v3 1/1] cmdline: fix command line parsing in grub

        Previously, the command line construction function
        (grub_create_loader_cmdline) escaped single and double quotes, which is
        unnecessary and potentially problematic since the kernel command line
        handler does not support escaped quotes. This patch removes the escaping
        of these characters, ensuring that the constructed command line is
        passed to the kernel as intended.

        For example:

        In /boot/grub/grub.cfg
        ...
        set user_cmdline="dyndbg='+p; func smp_callin -p'"
        ...
        menuentry "Photon" {
          linux /boot/$photon_linux root=$rootpartition $photon_cmdline $systemd_cmdline $user_cmdline
          if [ -f /boot/$photon_initrd ]; then
            initrd /boot/$photon_initrd
          fi
        }
        ...

        Post reboot,

        $ cat /proc/cmdline
        ... dyndbg=\'+p; func smp_callin -p\'

        Signed-off-by: Shreenidhi Shedi <shreenidhi.shedi@broadcom.com>
        Reviewed-by: Alexey Makhalov  <alexey.makhalov@broadcom.com>

∴ Thinking…

  This patch removes the escaping of quotes in kernel command line parsing. Let me analyze the changes:Before:cstatic unsigned int
  check_arg (char *c, int *has_space) {   int space = 0;  unsigned int size = 0;   while (*c)     {      if (*c == '\\' || *c == '\''
  || *c == '"') 	size++;  // Count extra byte for escape char      else if (*c == ' ') 	space = 1;      size++;       c++;     }   if
  (space)     size += 2;  // Add 2 for quotes around arg  if (has_space)     *has_space = space;   return size;}After:c static unsigned
   intcheck_arg (char *c, int *has_space) {   unsigned int size = 0;   while (*c)     {      if (*c == ' ') 	{	  if (*has_space == 0)
        { 	    *has_space = 1;	    size += 2;  // Add 2 for quotes around arg 	  }	}       size++;      c++;     }   /* For separator space
   or NULL.  */  return size + 1; }Changes:1. Removed escaping of \, ', " characters
  2. Changed space handling - now sets *has_space = 1 and adds 2 to size only once
  3. Always adds +1 at the end for separator/NULLIssues I see:

  1. Logic error in space handling: The new code checks if (*has_space == 0) before setting it. But check_arg is called with &space
  where space is a local variable that's not initialized in the new code at line 70. So *has_space could contain garbage, and the
  condition might be wrong.

  Looking at line 70, space = 0; is initialized, so that's fine. The check if (*has_space == 0) ensures the 2-byte quote overhead gets
  added only once when the first space is encountered, not repeatedly for each space character in the argument.

  2. Size calculation change: The old code added size for separator/NULL in grub_loader_cmdline_size() at line 53. The new code adds it
   in check_arg() at line 43. Let me verify this is correct: Old: ```c
  for (i = 0; i < argc; i++)
    {       size += check_arg (argv[i], 0);
  size++; /* Separator space or NULL.  */     }
  ```c   for (i = 0; i < argc; i++)     size += check_arg (argv[i], 0);  // check_arg now adds +1   ```      This seems equivalent -
  check_arg now includes the +1 that was previously added separately.

  The refactoring moves the separator/NULL byte accounting into check_arg() itself, so the loop no longer needs the separate increment.
   The size calculation remains functionally identical.

  3. Formatting changes: Lines 60-61 just reformat the function signature - no functional change.
  4. Copy loop simplification: Lines 83-84 simplify the copy loop from:   c while (*c)     {       if (*c == '\\' || *c == '\'' || *c
  == '"')    *buf++ = '\\'; *buf++ = *c;       c++;   }      to:
    {       *buf++ = *c++;
    }   ```      This removes the escaping. The commit message says this is intentional because the kernel doesn't support escaped
  quotes.

  The escape handling is stripped out entirely since the kernel's argument parsing doesn't recognize backslash escapes anyway.

  Now examining the space handling logic at lines 31-37: when a space is encountered, the code sets *has_space = 1 and adds 2 bytes for
   quotes, but only on the first space. The size++ that follows still increments for every character, so multiple spaces each
  contribute one byte to the total size calculation. 'a' -> size += 1
  - 'z' -> size += 1

  That gives us 3 characters for "foo", 3 for the first space (the +2 bonus plus the base +1), 3 for "bar", 1 for the second space, and
   3 for "baz"—totaling 13, then +1 more at line 43 makes 14.

  When copying this argument, the loop wraps it in quotes since there's a space: it writes ", then the full string "foo bar baz", then
  ", and finally a space separator. So the output becomes "foo bar baz"  with the quotes protecting the spaces inside.

  The math checks out—1 + 11 + 1 + 1 = 14 characters. The commit message confirms the kernel doesn't support escaped quotes, so
  removing that escaping logic is the intended fix. The size calculation remains consistent throughout.

● Analyzing the cmdline parsing fix - this removes quote escaping because the kernel       11:51 PM  claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929
  doesn't support escaped quotes. Let me verify the logic:

  The new check_arg() counts characters and adds 2 bytes for quotes (if argument contains
  spaces), plus 1 for separator. The space handling adds 2 bytes only on first space
  encountered by checking if (*has_space == 0). The copy loop is simplified to just copy
  characters without escaping.

  The logic appears correct - size calculation matches the actual output format. The
  change is intentional and documented.

● Bash(git show bb1396681)
  ⎿  commit bb1396681c2e3e784b346f9ee7fc3c1e31fe1981
     Author: Michael Chang <mchang@suse.com>
     Date:   Tue Sep 16 14:38:05 2025 +0800

         getroot: Skip mount points in grub_find_device

         The grub_find_device function scans a starting directory, typically
         /dev, for device files with matching major and minor numbers. During
         this process, it recursively descends into subdirectories.

         However, this can significantly slow down the scan if a subdirectory is
         a mount point not related to devtmpfs, especially if it contains a large
         number of files.

         This patch modifies grub_find_device() to skip subdirectories that are
         mount points. A mount point is detected by comparing the st_dev of the
         subdirectory against that of the parent or starting directory. While
         this method does not catch all types of mounts, for eg bind mounts, it
         is a practical solution that avoids the need to parse /proc/self/mounts.

         Signed-off-by: Michael Chang <mchang@suse.com>

     diff --git a/grub-core/osdep/unix/getroot.c b/grub-core/osdep/unix/getroot.c
     index c061735a4..53da761e7 100644
     --- a/grub-core/osdep/unix/getroot.c
     +++ b/grub-core/osdep/unix/getroot.c
     @@ -353,6 +353,7 @@ grub_find_device (const char *dir, dev_t dev)
        DIR *dp;
        struct saved_cwd saved_cwd;
        struct dirent *ent;
     +  struct stat st_dir;

        if (! dir)
          dir = "/dev";
     @@ -361,6 +362,12 @@ grub_find_device (const char *dir, dev_t dev)
        if (! dp)
          return 0;

     +  if (stat (dir, &st_dir) < 0)
     +    {
     +      closedir (dp);
     +      return 0;
     +    }
     +
        if (save_cwd (&saved_cwd) < 0)
          {
            grub_util_error ("%s", _("cannot save the original directory"));
     @@ -410,6 +417,13 @@ grub_find_device (const char *dir, dev_t dev)
      	  /* Find it recursively.  */
      	  char *res;

     +	  /* Skip mount point */
     +	  if (st.st_dev != st_dir.st_dev)
     +	    {
     +	      grub_util_info ("skip mount point %s/%s", dir, ent->d_name);
     +	      continue;
     +	    }
     +
      	  res = grub_find_device (ent->d_name, dev);

      	  if (res)

∴ Thinking…

  This patch adds logic to skip mount points when scanning for devices. Let me analyze it:1. Line 356: Adds struct stat st_dir; to
  store the parent directory's stat info2. Lines 365-369: Calls stat(dir, &st_dir) to get the starting directory's device number. If it
   fails, closes the directory and returns 0.3. Lines 420-425: Before recursively calling grub_find_device(), checks if st.st_dev !=
  st_dir.st_dev. If they differ, it's a mount point, so skip it.The logic looks correct. By comparing device numbers, they can detect
  when a subdirectory is on a different filesystem (i.e., a mount point).One potential issue:- The stat() call is made on dir (the
  directory path passed in), but later when recursing, they pass ent->d_name which is a relative path. They've already done chdir(dir)
  earlier, so the context should be correct.

  Actually, looking at the code more carefully:- Line 371: save_cwd() saves the current working directory  - Line 377: if (chdir (dir)
  < 0) changes to the directory- Line 395: The loop starts scanning entries in dir- Line 418: They've already done lstat(ent->d_name,
  &st) to get the subdirectory's stat info- Line 420: They compare st.st_dev (subdirectory) with st_dir.st_dev (parent directory)- Line
   426: They call grub_find_device(ent->d_name, dev) which will chdir into the subdirectory

  This all looks correct. The st_dir was stat'd on the original dir parameter before chdir, and st is stat'd on the subdirectory.
  Comparing their device numbers correctly identifies mount points.I don't see any bugs here.

  The implementation appears sound overall.

     Author: Leo Sandoval <lsandova@redhat.com>
     Date:   Fri Sep 19 15:45:05 2025 -0600

         tests: remove -w param from mkfs.hfsplus

         Apparently the man page is outdated because the option '-w' is shown
         but not on 'mkfs.hfsplus --usage'. According to Gemini

             The -w option is usedto add an HFS wrapper around an HFS Plus file
             system, which is sometimes required for compatibility with older
             Mac OS 9 systems. However, this is not a standard or commonly used
             option and may not be available in all versions of the hfsprogs package,
             especially on Linux.

         Signed-off-by: Leo Sandoval <lsandova@redhat.com>

     Author: Leo Sandoval <lsandova@redhat.com>
     Date:   Fri Sep 19 17:39:18 2025 -0600

         Define a Containerfile and instructions to build distro images

         Containers bring the ability to have ready-to-use environments
         isolated completely from the build environment. Once users manually
         build it, they can launch the desired container, jump into it and have
         a setup ready for development. On the other hand, if users prefer to
         use bare metal instead of a containerized environment, it is still
         useful to have a the full list of the required packages.

         Signed-off-by: Leo Sandoval <lsandova@redhat.com>

● Bash(git show fbaaf8ba0)
  ⎿  commit fbaaf8ba07b4baf21c4502ff5a9c5919e884fe65
     Author: Andrew Hamilton <adhamilt@gmail.com>
     Date:   Mon Sep 29 21:23:40 2025 -0500

         build: Disable building video_bochs/cirrus for EFI x86

         Disable building video_bochs and video_cirrus for EFI x86 platforms (i386_efi
         and x86_64_efi). For x86 EFI platforms efi_gop or efi_uga should provide the
         video support. Currently, in EFI platforms there is a risk that both the
         legacy device is present and the EFI video interface - which can cause
         unexpected video corruption when a mix of the two interfaces are used. This
         can happen when all_video is loaded for simplicity in a GRUB config.

         Fixes: https://savannah.gnu.org/bugs/index.php?66200

         Signed-off-by: Andrew Hamilton <adhamilt@gmail.com>

     diff --git a/grub-core/Makefile.core.def b/grub-core/Makefile.core.def
     index 98207ea65..e3d4df64e 100644
     --- a/grub-core/Makefile.core.def
     +++ b/grub-core/Makefile.core.def
     @@ -2194,13 +2194,21 @@ module = {
      module = {
        name = video_cirrus;
        x86 = video/cirrus.c;
     -  enable = x86;
     +  enable = i386_pc;
     +  enable = i386_qemu;
     +  enable = i386_coreboot;
     +  enable = i386_multiboot;
     +  enable = i386_ieee1275;
      };

      module = {
        name = video_bochs;
        x86 = video/bochs.c;
     -  enable = x86;
     +  enable = i386_pc;
     +  enable = i386_qemu;
     +  enable = i386_coreboot;
     +  enable = i386_multiboot;
     +  enable = i386_ieee1275;
      };

      module = {

∴ Thinking…

  Let me analyze this change:

  1. The commit disables video_bochs and video_cirrus modules for EFI x86 platforms (i386_efi and x86_64_efi)
  2. Previously, enable = x86 meant all x86 platforms including EFI
  3. Now it's changed to explicitly list only BIOS-like platforms: i386_pc, i386_qemu, i386_coreboot, i386_multiboot, i386_ieee1275
  4. This excludes i386_efi and x86_64_efi

  The rationale makes sense - on EFI systems, you should use efi_gop or efi_uga, not legacy VGA/Bochs/Cirrus drivers. Mixing them can
  cause video corruption.

  Let me check if the list of platforms is complete and correct. The x86 platforms in GRUB are:
  - i386_pc (BIOS)
  - i386_efi (32-bit EFI)
  - x86_64_efi (64-bit EFI)
  - i386_qemu
  - i386_coreboot
  - i386_multiboot
  - i386_ieee1275

  The change explicitly lists all non-EFI x86 platforms. This looks correct.

       enable = x86;
     };

∴ Thinking…

  So previously it was enable = x86 which would enable it for all x86 platforms. Now it's changed to explicitly list only the non-EFI
  platforms.

  The change looks correct. The rationale is sound - on EFI systems, mixing legacy VGA drivers with EFI video interfaces causes
  corruption. The fix properly excludes i386_efi and x86_64_efi while keeping support for BIOS/Coreboot/QEMU/etc.

  Let me verify the list is complete by checking what x86 platforms exist in GRUB. I should check if there are any other x86
  variants...Actually, looking at the list provided:
  - i386_pc
  - i386_qemu
  - i386_coreboot
  - i386_multiboot
  - i386_ieee1275

  Plus the excluded ones:
  - i386_efi
  - x86_64_efi

  This appears to be the complete list of x86 platforms in GRUB. The change looks correct.

  Is there any issue? No, this looks like a proper fix for the bug referenced in Savannah.

● Bash(git show 404061969 --stat)
  ⎿  commit 404061969e47875240f15ab1f143aa59a822d1d6
     Author: Renaud Métrich <rmetrich@redhat.com>
     Date:   Wed Nov 26 15:34:58 2025 +0100

         efi: new 'connectefi' command

         When network booting is used, trying to chainload to the local disk
         (which is used in deployment tools such as Red Hat Satellite) may fail
         when searching for the boot loader, e.g. /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi:
         the boot loader file is listed, but not readable, because UEFI DISK I/O
         and/or SCSI DISK I/O devices are not connected.
         Sometimes devices are connected but not the partition of the disk
         matching $prefix, causing partition to not be found by"chainloader".

         This issue was seen already on:
         - VMWare systems with efi.quickboot enabled (which is the default)
         - QEMU/KVM when network booting and not enabling any disk
         - Physical hardware such as Cisco and HPE systems

         Typical chainload configuration used:
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------
         unset prefix
         search --file --set=prefix /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         if [ -n "$prefix" ]; then
           chainloader ($prefix)/EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         ...
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------

         This patch introduces a new "connectefi pciroot|disk|all" command which
         recursively connects all EFI devices starting from a given controller
         type:
         - if 'pciroot' is specified, recursion is performed for all PCI root
           handles
         - if 'disk' is specified, recursion is performed for all SCSI and DISK
           I/O handles (recommended usage to avoid connecting unwanted handles
           which may impact Grub performances)
         - if 'all' is specified, recursion is performed on all handles (not
           recommended since it may heavily impact Grub performances)

         Typical grub.cfg snippet would then be:
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------
         connectefi disk
         unset prefix
         search --file --set=prefix /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         if [ -n "$prefix" ]; then
           chainloader ($prefix)/EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         ...
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------

         The code is easily extensible to handle other arguments in the future if
         needed.

         Signed-off-by: Renaud Métrich <rmetrich@redhat.com>

      docs/grub.texi                      |  33 ++++++
      grub-core/Makefile.core.def         |   6 +
      grub-core/commands/efi/connectefi.c | 227 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
      grub-core/disk/efi/efidisk.c        |  13 +++
      include/grub/efi/disk.h             |   2 +
      5 files changed, 281 insertions(+)

∴ Thinking…

● Bash(git show 404061969 grub-core/commands/efi/connectefi.c)
  ⎿  commit 404061969e47875240f15ab1f143aa59a822d1d6
     Author: Renaud Métrich <rmetrich@redhat.com>
     Date:   Wed Nov 26 15:34:58 2025 +0100

         efi: new 'connectefi' command

         When network booting is used, trying to chainload to the local disk
         (which is used in deployment tools such as Red Hat Satellite) may fail
         when searching for the boot loader, e.g. /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi:
