         and/or SCSI DISK I/O devices are not connected.
         Sometimes devices are connected but not the partition of the disk
         matching $prefix, causing partition to not be found by"chainloader".

         This issue was seen already on:
         - VMWare systems with efi.quickboot enabled (which is the default)
         - QEMU/KVM when network booting and not enabling any disk
         - Physical hardware such as Cisco and HPE systems

         Typical chainload configuration used:
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------
         unset prefix
         search --file --set=prefix /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         if [ -n "$prefix" ]; then
           chainloader ($prefix)/EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         ...
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------

         This patch introduces a new "connectefi pciroot|disk|all" command which
         recursively connects all EFI devices starting from a given controller
         type:
         - if 'pciroot' is specified, recursion is performed for all PCI root
           handles
         - if 'disk' is specified, recursion is performed for all SCSI and DISK
           I/O handles (recommended usage to avoid connecting unwanted handles
           which may impact Grub performances)

           recommended since it may heavily impact Grub performances)

         Typical grub.cfg snippet would then be:
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------
         connectefi disk
         unset prefix
         search --file --set=prefix /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         if [ -n "$prefix" ]; then
           chainloader ($prefix)/EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         ...
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------

         The code is easily extensible to handle other arguments in the future if
         needed.

         Signed-off-by: Renaud Métrich <rmetrich@redhat.com>

     diff --git a/grub-core/commands/efi/connectefi.c b/grub-core/commands/efi/connectefi.c
     new file mode 100644
     index 000000000..61a3d0efb
     --- /dev/null
     +++ b/grub-core/commands/efi/connectefi.c
     @@ -0,0 +1,227 @@
     +/*
     + *  GRUB  --  GRand Unified Bootloader
     + *  Copyright (C) 2025  Free Software Foundation, Inc.
     + *
     + *  GRUB is free software: you can redistribute it and/or modify
     + *  it under the terms of the GNU General Public License as published by
     + *  the Free Software Foundation, either version 3 of the License, or
     + *  (at your option) any later version.
     + *
     + *  GRUB is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
     + *  but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
     + *  MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
     + *  GNU General Public License for more details.
     + *
     + *  You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
     + *  along with GRUB.  If not, see <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/>.
     + */
     +
     +#include <grub/types.h>
     +#include <grub/mm.h>
     +#include <grub/misc.h>
     +#include <grub/efi/api.h>
     +#include <grub/efi/pci.h>
     +#include <grub/efi/efi.h>
     +#include <grub/efi/disk.h>
     +#include <grub/command.h>
     +#include <grub/err.h>
     +#include <grub/i18n.h>
     +
     +GRUB_MOD_LICENSE ("GPLv3+");
     +
     +struct grub_efi_handle_list
     +{
     +  struct grub_efi_handle_list *next;
     +  struct grub_efi_handle_list **prev;
     +  grub_efi_handle_t handle;
     +};
     +typedef struct grub_efi_handle_list grub_efi_handle_list_t;
     +
     +enum search_item_flag
     +{
     +  SEARCH_NONE = 0,
     +  SEARCH_LOOP = 1,
     +  SEARCH_RECURSIVE = 2
     +};
     +typedef enum search_item_flag search_item_flag_t;
     +
     +struct search_item
     +{
     +  grub_guid_t guid;
     +  const char *name;
     +  search_item_flag_t flags;
     +};
     +typedef struct search_item search_item_t;
     +
     +static bool
     +is_in_list (grub_efi_handle_t handle, grub_efi_handle_list_t *handles)
     +{
     +  grub_efi_handle_list_t *e;
     +
     +  FOR_LIST_ELEMENTS (e, handles)
     +    if (e->handle == handle)
     +      return true;
     +
     +  return false;
     +}
     +
     +static void
     +free_handle_list (grub_efi_handle_list_t **handles_p)
     +{
     +  grub_efi_handle_list_t *e, *n;
     +
     +  FOR_LIST_ELEMENTS_SAFE (e, n, *handles_p)
     +    grub_free (e);
     +
     +  *handles_p = NULL;
     +}
     +
     +static grub_err_t
     +grub_cmd_connectefi (grub_command_t cmd __attribute__ ((unused)),
     +		     int argc, char **args)
     +{
     +  search_item_t pciroot_items[] =
     +    {
     +      { GRUB_EFI_PCI_ROOT_IO_GUID, "PCI root", SEARCH_RECURSIVE }
     +    };
     +  search_item_t disk_items[] =
     +    {
     +      { GRUB_EFI_PCI_ROOT_IO_GUID, "PCI root", SEARCH_NONE },
     +      { GRUB_EFI_PCI_IO_GUID, "PCI", SEARCH_LOOP },
     +      { GRUB_EFI_SCSI_IO_PROTOCOL_GUID, "SCSI I/O", SEARCH_RECURSIVE },
     +      { GRUB_EFI_DISK_IO_GUID, "DISK I/O", SEARCH_RECURSIVE }
     +    };
     +  search_item_t *items = NULL;
     +  int i, nitems = 0;
     +  grub_err_t err = GRUB_ERR_NONE;
     +  bool reenumerate_disks = false;
     +  grub_efi_handle_list_t *already_handled = NULL;
     +  grub_efi_handle_t *handles = NULL;
     +
     +  if (argc != 1)
     +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("one argument expected"));
     +
     +  if (grub_strcmp (args[0], "pciroot") == 0)
     +    {
     +      items = pciroot_items;
     +      nitems = ARRAY_SIZE (pciroot_items);
     +    }
     +  else if (grub_strcmp (args[0], "disk") == 0)
     +    {
     +      items = disk_items;
     +      nitems = ARRAY_SIZE (disk_items);
     +    }
     +  else if (grub_strcmp (args[0], "all") == 0)
     +    {
     +      items = NULL;
     +      nitems = 1;
     +    }
     +  else
     +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT,
     +		       N_("unexpected argument: `%s'"), args[0]);
     +
     +  for (i = 0; i < nitems; i++)
     +    {
     +      grub_efi_uintn_t num_handles, j;
     +      int loop = 0;
     +      const int max_loop_depth = 100;
     +      bool continue_search;
     +
     +      do
     +	{
     +	  continue_search = false;
     +	  grub_dprintf ("efi", "item: %s loop: %d\n", (items != NULL) ? items[i].name: "ALL", loop);
     +	  if (items != NULL)
     +	    {
     +	      if (++loop >= max_loop_depth)
     +		{
     +		  err = grub_error (GRUB_ERR_RECURSION_DEPTH,
     +			"connectefi: Maximum recursion depth exceeded for item '%s'",
     +			items[i].name);
     +		  goto fail;
     +		}
     +	      handles = grub_efi_locate_handle (GRUB_EFI_BY_PROTOCOL,
     +					        &items[i].guid, NULL, &num_handles);
     +	    }
     +	  else
     +	    handles = grub_efi_locate_handle (GRUB_EFI_ALL_HANDLES,
     +					      NULL, NULL, &num_handles);
     +
     +	  if (handles == NULL)
     +	    break; /* proceed to next item */
     +
     +	  for (j = 0; j < num_handles; j++)
     +	    {
     +	      grub_efi_handle_t handle = handles[j];
     +	      grub_efi_status_t status;
     +	      grub_efi_handle_list_t *item;
     +
     +	      /* Skip already handled handles */
     +	      if (is_in_list (handle, already_handled))
     +		{
     +		  grub_dprintf ("efi", "  handle %p: already processed\n", handle);
     +		  continue;
     +		}
     +
     +	      status = grub_efi_system_table->boot_services->connect_controller (
     +			handle, NULL, NULL,
     +			(items == NULL) || items[i].flags & SEARCH_RECURSIVE ? 1 : 0);
     +	      if (status == GRUB_EFI_SUCCESS)
     +		{
     +		  if (items != NULL && (items[i].flags & SEARCH_LOOP))
     +		    continue_search = true;
     +		  reenumerate_disks = true;
     +		  grub_dprintf ("efi", "  handle %p: connected\n", handle);
     +		}
     +	      else
     +		grub_dprintf ("efi", "  handle %p: failed to connect (%"
     +			      PRIxGRUB_EFI_UINTN_T ")\n", handle, status);
     +
     +	      item = grub_malloc (sizeof (*item));
     +	      if (item == NULL)
     +		{
     +		  err = grub_errno;
     +		  goto fail; /* fatal */
     +		}
     +	      grub_list_push (GRUB_AS_LIST_P (&already_handled), GRUB_AS_LIST (item));
     +	      item->handle = handle;
     +	    }
     +
     +	  grub_free (handles);
     +	  handles = NULL;
     +	}
     +      while (continue_search == true);
     +
     +      /* We are done with this item, proceed to next one */
     +      free_handle_list (&already_handled);
     +    }
     +
     + fail:
     +  grub_free (handles);
     +  free_handle_list (&already_handled);
     +
     +  if (reenumerate_disks)
     +    grub_efidisk_reenumerate_disks ();
     +
     +  return err;
     +}
     +
     +static grub_command_t cmd;
     +
     +GRUB_MOD_INIT(connectefi)
     +{
     +  cmd = grub_register_command ("connectefi", grub_cmd_connectefi,
     +			       "pciroot|disk|all",
     +			       N_("Connect EFI handles."
     +				  " If 'pciroot' is specified, connect PCI"
     +				  " root EFI handles recursively."
     +				  " If 'disk' is specified, connect SCSI"
     +				  " and DISK I/O EFI handles recursively."
     +				  " If 'all' is specified, connect all"
     +				  " EFI handles recursively."));
     +}
     +
     +GRUB_MOD_FINI(connectefi)
     +{
     +  grub_unregister_command (cmd);
     +}

∴ Thinking…

  Let me carefully review this code for issues:

  1. Line 68-75: free_handle_list() - properly frees all elements in linked list
  2. Line 98: grub_efi_handle_list_t *already_handled = NULL; - initialized properly
  3. Line 99: grub_efi_handle_t *handles = NULL; - initialized properly
  4. Line 134-140: Loop protection with max_loop_depth = 100
  5. Line 142-146: grub_efi_locate_handle() call - returns allocated memory
  6. Line 148-149: If handles is NULL, break and proceed to next item - OK
  7. Line 151-186: Main loop processing handles
  8. Line 157-162: Skip already handled handles - good
  9. Line 164-166: Connect controller call
  10. Line 178-184: Allocate new list item and add to already_handled list
  11. Line 180-183: If malloc fails, goto fail - good error handling
  12. Line 188-189: Free handles after each inner loop iteration
  13. Line 193: Free already_handled list after each item
  14. Line 196-198: Cleanup on failure
  15. Line 200-201: Re-enumerate disks if connections were made

  Wait, I see an issue at line 188:
  grub_free (handles);
  handles = NULL;

  This is inside the do-while loop that continues when continue_search == true. So after the first iteration, handles is freed and set
  to NULL. On the next iteration, line 142-146 will allocate new memory and assign to handles. This is OK.

  But wait - at line 196-198:
  fail:
    grub_free (handles);
    free_handle_list (&already_handled);



  If we jump to fail from line 139-141 or 180-183, handles may or may not be set. At line 139-141, we're inside the loop after ++loop
  >= max_loop_depth, so handles is either NULL (from initialization or previous cleanup at line 188) or contains the current
  allocation—either way, grub_free() handles it safely. At line 180-183, malloc fails while handles is already allocated, so freeing it
   is correct.


  c
  free_handle_list(&already_handled);  // cleared after processing each item

  At the error path, both allocations get cleaned up. The handles pointer either remains NULL or holds an allocated block that gets
  freed, and already_handled is also freed. Looking back at line 193, already_handled is reset after each item in the loop, which
  suggests it's meant to track handles within a single item's processing rather than across all items. For "pciroot" there's one item
  with recursive search, while "disk" has multiple items like PCI root, PCI, and SCSI. safe to call multiple times on the same handle.
  So this shouldn't cause problems, just redundant work.

  The key insight is that each protocol type (PCI root, PCI, SCSI I/O, DISK I/O) gets its own search strategy. PCI root doesn't recurse
   at all, PCI loops until saturation, while SCSI and DISK I/O both recurse through the entire tree. Since handles can implement
  multiple protocols, they may get connected multiple times across different item iterations, but that's acceptable given the
  idempotent nature of the operation. _handle (GRUB_EFI_BY_PROTOCOL, &items[i].guid, NULL, &num_handles);

  Each protocol search returns only handles implementing that specific protocol, so clearing already_handled between items shouldn't
  cause duplicates—PCI root handles won't appear in SCSI searches unless they implement both protocols, which is unlikely. Since
  connect_controller is idempotent anyway, even overlap wouldn't break anything. The loop depth is capped at 100, which seems
  reasonable for preventing infinite recursion.

         normal/menu_entry: Add grub_calloc failure check

         Add a NULL check to prevent failure from grub_calloc in update_screen.
         This stops possible NULL pointer dereference and avoids unexpected behavior.

         Signed-off-by: Sridhar Markonda <sridharm@linux.ibm.com>

     diff --git a/grub-core/normal/menu_entry.c b/grub-core/normal/menu_entry.c
     index 8b0d17e3f..411b0ad53 100644
     --- a/grub-core/normal/menu_entry.c
     +++ b/grub-core/normal/menu_entry.c
     @@ -295,8 +295,12 @@ update_screen (struct screen *screen, struct per_term_screen *term_screen,

      	  pos = linep->pos + (term_screen - screen->terms);

     -	  if (!*pos)
     -	    *pos = grub_calloc (linep->len + 1, sizeof (**pos));
     +	  if (*pos == NULL)
     +	    {
     +	      *pos = grub_calloc (linep->len + 1, sizeof (**pos));
     +	      if (*pos == NULL)
     +		return;
     +	    }

      	  if (i == region_start || linep == screen->lines + screen->line
      	      || (i > region_start && mode == ALL_LINES))

∴ Thinking…

  This adds a NULL check after grub_calloc. Let me verify this is correct.

  Looking at the code:
  - Line 298 (old): if (!*pos) - checks if the value pointed to by pos is NULL
  - Line 298-303 (new): Same check, but if grub_calloc returns NULL, return early

  This looks correct. The function should exit if allocation fails instead of dereferencing a NULL pointer.The commit message says "Add
   a NULL check to prevent failure from grub_calloc in update_screen" which is accurate.

  Is there any issue? Let me check what happens if we return early:
  - The function update_screen returns void
  - Calling code won't know allocation failed
  - But grub_calloc sets grub_errno on failure, so the error should be handled by the caller

  Actually, I should check if there's a memory leak. Let me see if there's any cleanup needed before returning...

  Looking at the function context, update_screen is called repeatedly to update the display. If allocation fails, returning early means
   the screen won't be updated, but that's better than crashing. The caller should check grub_errno.

  This looks like a straightforward bug fix with no issues.

∴ Thinking…

  Let me analyze this change:

  1. Adds a new icon_offset field to the list structure (line 43)
  2. Initializes it (presumably initialized to 0 by default in the constructor)
  3. Updates text_left_offset calculation to include icon_offset (line 369)
  4. Updates icon rendering position to include icon_offset (line 430)
  5. Adds property setter for "icon_offset" (lines 711-714)

  This looks straightforward. The offset is added to both the icon position and the text position (which depends on icon width).

  Let me check if there are any issues:

  1. Is icon_offset initialized? Looking at the diff, it's added as a struct field. In C, static/global structs are zero-initialized,
  and if this is heap-allocated with grub_zalloc, it would also be zero-initialized. Let me check if there's a constructor...

  Actually, I can't see the constructor in this diff. But assuming it's either zero-initialized or there's a constructor that sets it
  to 0, this should be fine. A default value of 0 means no offset, which maintains backward compatibility.

  2. Is the offset used correctly? Yes - it's added to both the icon position (line 430) and the text position (line 369). This makes
  sense - if icons are offset to the right, text should also be offset.
  3. Is there any validation of the offset value? No - grub_strtol can return negative values. If someone sets icon_offset to -100,
  icons and text would be drawn off-screen to the left. But this is probably acceptable - theme authors can test their themes. GRUB
  doesn't typically validate theme values extensively.
  4. Error handling? grub_strtol doesn't fail - it returns 0 if the string is invalid. This is acceptable.

  I don't see any bugs in this code. It's a simple feature addition with proper integration.

● Bash(git show --stat f8057e6b5)
  ⎿  commit f8057e6b53a09c421783be06db525666755fa1e7
     Author: Radoslav Kolev <radoslav.kolev@suse.com>
     Date:   Fri Dec 12 11:00:49 2025 +0200

         blsuki: scan XBOOTLDR partition for BLS/UKIs if it exists

         The default ESP partition on some systems is limited in
         space and storing a number of kernel and initrd images
         can fill it up quickly.

         It's not always practical to increase the size of the
         existing ESP, so the Boot Loader Spec defines a new Extended
         Boot Loader Partition (XBOOTLDR) with GPT type GUID of
         bc13c2ff-59e6-4262-a352-b275fd6f7172 to be searched for BLS
         enties and UKIs in addition to the ESP.

         see https://uapi-group.org/specifications/specs/boot_loader_specification/

         Signed-off-by: Radoslav Kolev <radoslav.kolev@suse.com>

      grub-core/commands/blsuki.c  | 86 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++----
      include/grub/gpt_partition.h |  7 ++++
      2 files changed, 86 insertions(+), 7 deletions(-)

∴ Thinking…

  This is a larger change. Let me read the implementation.

     +      *xbootldr_part = grub_xasprintf("%s,%s", disk->name, grub_partition_get_name(p));
     +      grub_dprintf("blsuki","XBOOTLDR found! setting name to %s\n", *xbootldr_part);
     +      return 1;
     +    }
     +  return 0;
     +}

∴ Thinking…

  Let me analyze this code carefully for issues:

  1. Line 1249: static const grub_guid_t xbootldr_type = GRUB_GPT_PARTITION_TYPE_XBOOTLDR; - OK, static const global
  2. Line 1250: static int find_xbootldr_iter (grub_disk_t disk, const grub_partition_t p, void *data) - callback function
  3. Line 1251-1252: Variable declarations
  4. Line 1256-1263: Read GPT partition entry data. This saves and restores disk->partition, which is good.
  5. Line 1265-1271: If GUID matches XBOOTLDR, allocate string with grub_xasprintf and return 1 to stop iteration. The allocated string
   is stored in *xbootldr_part.
  6. Line 1288: char *xbootldr_part = NULL; - initialized to NULL
  7. Line 1361-1366: First search on ESP partition (unchanged from original)
  8. Line 1372: Check if devid == NULL (no device specified)
  9. Line 1375: grub_disk_t esp_disk = grub_disk_open(grub_env_get ("root")); - opens disk
  10. Line 1377-1405: If disk opened successfully:
  - Line 1380: grub_free (esp_disk->partition); - POTENTIAL ISSUE: This frees the partition structure. But we need to check if it's
  NULL first. Actually, looking at the code, this is intentional - it's clearing the partition so we can access the raw disk. But this
  might leak memory if the partition field wasn't allocated separately. Actually, in GRUB, disk->partition points to a partition
  structure that should be freed when closing the disk, not manually. This looks wrong.
  - Line 1382: Sets partition to NULL to access the raw disk, then calls grub_gpt_partition_map_iterate to search for XBOOTLDR
  partitions. If the iteration fails, it logs a debug message and jumps to cleanup. When a matching partition is found, the code
  searches within it for boot entries before closing the disk.
  - There's a potential issue: manually freeing the partition structure at line 1380 with grub_free (esp_disk->partition) could cause
  problems when grub_disk_close() later attempts to free or access that same memory, leading to a double-free or use-after-free
  vulnerability.

● Write(/home/lpcs/lpcsf-new/test/rhel/packages/grub2/grub-devel/reviews/2025-12-0030.md)
  ⎿  Wrote 6 lines to /home/lpcs/lpcsf-new/test/rhel/packages/grub2/grub-devel/reviews/2025-12-0030.md
     # AI Review: MR !68 - Add XBOOTLDR partition support

     2 commits: Adds support for Extended Boot Loader Partition (XBOOTLDR) to store BLS entries/UKIs when ESP is full.

     - **Double-free risk** (grub-core/commands/blsuki.c:1380,1407 in commit 1): Code calls `grub_free(esp_disk->partition)` then
     later `grub_disk_close(esp_disk)`. The close function will attempt to free the partition again, causing double-free. Should
     use `grub_partition_t saved = esp_disk->partition; esp_disk->partition = NULL;` without freeing, then restore before closing.

● Bash(git show efd303f3a --stat)
  ⎿  commit efd303f3a4daf8b2a545903617d02601a7a03a78
     Author: Gary Lin <glin@suse.com>
     Date:   Fri Jan 16 14:12:50 2026 +0800

         key_protector: Add MFA key protector

         This patch introduces the MFA key protector to provide multi-factor
         authentication for encrypted partitions.

         The MFA protector does not store the disk key itself; instead, it relies
         on a split-key architecture where the disk key is divided into two parts
         stored in different underlying key protectors. When unlocking the
         partition, the module retrieves partial keys from specified protectors
         and derives the final disk key.

         To specify the underlying key protectors, use 'mfa_key_protector_init'
         with options --protector1 (or -1) and --protector2 (or -2). The sequence
         is crucial: the final key is reconstructed by combining intermediate keys
         in the exact order specified.

         Additionally, the 'mfa' module provides two basic key protectors:
         * password: Prompts the user for input.
         * file: Retrieves the key from a specified file path.

         Example: (file + password):

         - Create a combined disk key:

           $ dd if=/dev/random of=keyfile.bin bs=1 count=64
           $ echo -n "TOP SECRET" > user-pass.txt
           $ cat keyfile.bin user-pass.txt > luks-key.bin

         - Enroll the key into LUKS:

           # cryptsetup luksAddKey --new-keyfile luks-key.bin /dev/disk/by-uuid/...

         - GRUB commands:

           file_key_protector_init -k (hd1,gpt1)/keyfile/keyfile.bin
           mfa_key_protector_init -1 file -2 password
           cryptomount -u <UUID> -P mfa

         The result:
           Retrieving MFA key 1 from 'file' key protector
           Retrieving MFA key 2 from 'password' key protector
           Enter MFA passphrase:
           Attempting to decrypt master key...
           Slot "1" opened

         The MFA key protector fetches the first half of the key from the key
         file and requests the passphrase as the second half. With the full disk
         key, GRUB unlocks the partition successfully.

         Signed-off-by: Gary Lin <glin@suse.com>

      grub-core/Makefile.core.def |   5 +
      grub-core/commands/mfa.c    | 576 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
      2 files changed, 581 insertions(+)

∴ Thinking…

  This is a large new feature. Let me read the implementation carefully to look for bugs.

     Author: Gary Lin <glin@suse.com>
     Date:   Fri Jan 16 14:12:50 2026 +0800

         key_protector: Add MFA key protector

         This patch introduces the MFA key protector to provide multi-factor
         authentication for encrypted partitions.

         The MFA protector does not store the disk key itself; instead, it relies
         on a split-key architecture where the disk key is divided into two parts
         stored in different underlying key protectors. When unlocking the
         partition, the module retrieves partial keys from specified protectors
         and derives the final disk key.

         To specify the underlying key protectors, use 'mfa_key_protector_init'
         with options --protector1 (or -1) and --protector2 (or -2). The sequence
         is crucial: the final key is reconstructed by combining intermediate keys
         in the exact order specified.

         Additionally, the 'mfa' module provides two basic key protectors:
         * password: Prompts the user for input.
         * file: Retrieves the key from a specified file path.

         Example: (file + password):

         - Create a combined disk key:

           $ dd if=/dev/random of=keyfile.bin bs=1 count=64
           $ echo -n "TOP SECRET" > user-pass.txt
           $ cat keyfile.bin user-pass.txt > luks-key.bin

         - Enroll the key into LUKS:

           # cryptsetup luksAddKey --new-keyfile luks-key.bin /dev/disk/by-uuid/...

         - GRUB commands:

           file_key_protector_init -k (hd1,gpt1)/keyfile/keyfile.bin
           mfa_key_protector_init -1 file -2 password
           cryptomount -u <UUID> -P mfa

         The result:
           Retrieving MFA key 1 from 'file' key protector
           Retrieving MFA key 2 from 'password' key protector
           Enter MFA passphrase:
           Attempting to decrypt master key...
           Slot "1" opened

         The MFA key protector fetches the first half of the key from the key
         file and requests the passphrase as the second half. With the full disk
         key, GRUB unlocks the partition successfully.

         Signed-off-by: Gary Lin <glin@suse.com>

● Bash(git show efd303f3a grub-core/commands/mfa.c | sed -n '200,400p')
  ⎿  +static password_context_t password_ctx = {0};

     +static grub_extcmd_t file_init_cmd;

     +static grub_err_t
     +mfa_recover_key (grub_uint8_t **key, grub_size_t *key_size)
     +{
     +  grub_uint8_t *p1_key = NULL, *p2_key = NULL;
     +  grub_size_t p1_size, p2_size, total_size;
     +  grub_err_t err;
     +
     +  if (mfa_ctx.protector1 == NULL || mfa_ctx.protector2 == NULL)
     +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("MFA key protector(s) not set"));
     +
     +  grub_printf_ (N_("Retrieving MFA key 1 from '%s' key protector\n"), mfa_ctx.protector1);
     +  err = grub_key_protector_recover_key (mfa_ctx.protector1, &p1_key, &p1_size);
     +  if (err != GRUB_ERR_NONE)
     +    goto error;
     +
     +  grub_printf_ (N_("Retrieving MFA key 2 from '%s' key protector\n"), mfa_ctx.protector2);
     +  err = grub_key_protector_recover_key (mfa_ctx.protector2, &p2_key, &p2_size);
     +  if (err != GRUB_ERR_NONE)
     +    goto error;
     +
     +  total_size = p1_size + p2_size;
     +  if (total_size < p1_size || total_size < p2_size || total_size == 0)
     +    {
     +      err = grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_NUMBER, N_("invalid MFA key size"));
     +      goto error;
     +    }
     +
     +  *key_size = total_size;
     +  *key = grub_malloc (*key_size);
     +  if (*key == NULL)
     +    {
     +      err = GRUB_ERR_OUT_OF_MEMORY;
     +      goto error;
     +    }
     +
     +  grub_memcpy (*key, p1_key, p1_size);
     +  grub_memcpy (*key + p1_size, p2_key, p2_size);
     +
     +  err = GRUB_ERR_NONE;
     +
     +error:
     +  /* Erase the intermediate keys */
     +  if (p1_key != NULL)
     +    {
     +      grub_memset (p1_key, 0, p1_size);
     +      grub_free (p1_key);
     +    }
     +  if (p2_key != NULL)
     +    {
     +      grub_memset (p2_key, 0, p2_size);
     +      grub_free (p2_key);
     +    }
     +
     +  return err;
     +}
     +
     +static grub_err_t
     +password_recover_key (grub_uint8_t **key, grub_size_t *key_size)
     +{
     +  char password[GRUB_CRYPTODISK_MAX_PASSPHRASE];
     +  grub_err_t err;
     +
     +  if (password_ctx.password_len != 0)
     +    {
     +      *key = password_ctx.password;
     +      *key_size = password_ctx.password_len;
     +      return GRUB_ERR_NONE;
     +    }
     +
     +  grub_printf_ (N_("Enter MFA passphrase: "));
     +  if (grub_password_get (password , GRUB_CRYPTODISK_MAX_PASSPHRASE) == 0)
     +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("passphrase not supplied"));
     +
     +  *key_size = grub_strlen (password);
     +  *key = grub_malloc (*key_size);
     +  if (*key == NULL)
     +    {
     +      err = GRUB_ERR_OUT_OF_MEMORY;
     +      goto error;
     +    }
     +
     +  grub_memcpy (*key, password, *key_size);
     +
     +  err = GRUB_ERR_NONE;
     +
     +error:
     +  grub_memset (password, 0, GRUB_CRYPTODISK_MAX_PASSPHRASE);
     +
     +  return err;
     +}
     +
     +static grub_err_t
     +file_recover_key (grub_uint8_t **key, grub_size_t *key_size)
     +{
     +  const char *filepath;
     +  grub_file_t file;
     +  grub_off_t file_size;
     +  void *read_buffer;
     +  grub_off_t read_n;
     +  grub_err_t err;
     +
     +  if (file_ctx.keyfile == NULL)
     +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("file path not supplied"));
     +
     +  filepath = file_ctx.keyfile;
     +
     +  file = grub_file_open (filepath, GRUB_FILE_TYPE_CRYPTODISK_ENCRYPTION_KEY);
     +  if (file == NULL)
     +    {
     +      /* Push errno from grub_file_open() into the error message stack */
     +      grub_error_push ();
     +      err = grub_error (GRUB_ERR_FILE_NOT_FOUND, N_("could not open file: %s"), filepath);
     +      goto error;
     +    }
     +
     +  file_size = grub_file_size (file);
     +  if (file_size == 0)
     +    {
     +      err = grub_error (GRUB_ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE, N_("could not read file size: %s"), filepath);
     +      goto error;
     +    }
     +
     +  if (file_ctx.keyfile_offset > file_size)
     +    {
     +      err = grub_error (GRUB_ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE,
     +			N_("Keyfile offset, %llu, is greater than keyfile size, %llu"),
     +			   file_ctx.keyfile_offset, (unsigned long long) file_size);
     +      goto error;
     +    }
     +
     +
     +  if (file_ctx.keyfile_size != 0)
     +    {
     +      if (file_ctx.keyfile_size > (file_size - file_ctx.keyfile_offset))
     +	{
     +	  err = grub_error (GRUB_ERR_FILE_READ_ERROR,
     +			    N_("keyfile is too small: requested %llu bytes,"
     +			       " but the file only has %llu bytes left at offset %llu"),
     +			    file_ctx.keyfile_size,
     +			    (unsigned long long) (file_size - file_ctx.keyfile_offset),
     +			    file_ctx.keyfile_offset);
     +	  goto error;
     +	}
     +      *key_size = file_ctx.keyfile_size;
     +    }
     +  else
     +    *key_size = file_size;
     +
     +  if (grub_file_seek (file, (grub_off_t) file_ctx.keyfile_offset) == (grub_off_t) -1)
     +    {
     +      err = grub_errno;
     +      goto error;
     +    }
     +
     +  read_buffer = grub_malloc (*key_size);
     +  if (read_buffer == NULL)
     +    {
     +      err = grub_error (GRUB_ERR_OUT_OF_MEMORY, N_("could not allocate buffer for %s"), filepath);
     +      goto error;
     +    }
     +
     +  read_n = grub_file_read (file, read_buffer, *key_size);
     +  if (read_n != *key_size)
     +    {
     +      grub_free (read_buffer);
     +      err = grub_error (GRUB_ERR_FILE_READ_ERROR, N_("could not retrieve file contents: %s"), filepath);
     +      goto error;
     +    }
     +
     +  *key = read_buffer;
     +
     +  err = GRUB_ERR_NONE;
     +
     + error:
     +  if (file != NULL)
     +    grub_file_close (file);
     +
     +  return err;
     +}
     +
     +static grub_err_t
     +mfa_init_cmd_handler (grub_extcmd_context_t ctxt, int argc,
     +		      char **args __attribute__ ((unused)))
     +{
     +  struct grub_arg_list *state = ctxt->state;
     +  grub_err_t err;
     +
     +  if (argc > 0)
     +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT,
     +		       N_("the MFA key protector does not accept any non-option arguments "
     +			  "(i.e., like -o and/or --option only)"));
     +
     +  grub_free ((void *) mfa_ctx.protector1);
     +  grub_free ((void *) mfa_ctx.protector2);
     +  grub_memset (&mfa_ctx, 0, sizeof (mfa_ctx));

∴ Thinking…

  Let me continue reviewing. I'm looking at the key recovery functions. I notice:

  1. Line 209-263: mfa_recover_key() - This looks OK. It retrieves two keys, checks for overflow, combines them, and properly erases
  the intermediate keys.
  2. Line 265-303: password_recover_key() - This returns a pointer to password_ctx.password if already set (line 273), or prompts for
  password. This looks OK, but I need to check if there's any double-free risk.
  3. Line 305-390: file_recover_key() - Reads key from file. Error handling looks good.

  Let me look at the rest of the file to check initialization and cleanup.

    +static grub_err_t
    +mfa_clear_cmd_handler (grub_extcmd_context_t ctxt __attribute__ ((unused)),
    +		       int argc, char **args __attribute__ ((unused)))
    +{
    +  if (argc > 0)
    +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("mfa_key_protector_clear accepts no arguments"));
    +
    +  grub_free ((void *) mfa_ctx.protector1);
    +  grub_free ((void *) mfa_ctx.protector2);
    +  grub_memset (&mfa_ctx, 0, sizeof (mfa_ctx));
    +
    +  return GRUB_ERR_NONE;
    +}
    +
    +static grub_err_t
    +password_init_cmd_handler (grub_extcmd_context_t ctxt, int argc,
    +			   char **args __attribute__ ((unused)))
    +{
    +  struct grub_arg_list *state = ctxt->state;
    +
    +  if (argc > 0)
    +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("the password key protector does not accept any non-option arguments (i.e.,
    like -o and/or --option only)"));
    +
    +  grub_free ((void *) password_ctx.password);
    +  grub_memset (&password_ctx, 0, sizeof (password_ctx));
    +
    +  if (state[OPTION_PASSWORD].set)  /* password */
    +    {
    +      password_ctx.password = (grub_uint8_t *) grub_strdup (state[OPTION_PASSWORD].arg);
    +      password_ctx.password_len = grub_strlen (state[OPTION_PASSWORD].arg);
    +    }
    +
    +  /* This command only initializes the protector, so nothing else to do. */
    +
    +  return GRUB_ERR_NONE;
    +}
    +
    +static grub_err_t
    +password_clear_cmd_handler (grub_extcmd_context_t ctxt __attribute__ ((unused)),
    +			    int argc, char **args __attribute__ ((unused)))
    +{
    +  if (argc > 0)
    +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("pw_key_protector_clear accepts no arguments"));
    +
    +  grub_free ((void *) password_ctx.password);
    +  grub_memset (&password_ctx, 0, sizeof (password_ctx));
    +
    +  return GRUB_ERR_NONE;
    +}
    +
    +static grub_err_t
    +file_init_cmd_handler (grub_extcmd_context_t ctxt, int argc,
    +		       char **args __attribute__ ((unused)))
    +{
    +  struct grub_arg_list *state = ctxt->state;
    +  unsigned long long keyfile_offset = 0, keyfile_size = 0;
    +  const char *p = NULL;
    +
    +  if (argc > 0)
    +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("the file key protector does not accept any non-option arguments (i.e., like
     -o and/or --option only)"));
    +
    +  grub_free ((void *) file_ctx.keyfile);
    +  grub_memset (&file_ctx, 0, sizeof (file_ctx));
    +
    +  if (state[OPTION_KEYFILE_OFFSET].set) /* keyfile-offset */
    +    {
    +      grub_errno = GRUB_ERR_NONE;
    +      keyfile_offset = grub_strtoull (state[OPTION_KEYFILE_OFFSET].arg, &p, 0);
    +
    +      if (state[OPTION_KEYFILE_OFFSET].arg[0] == '\0' || *p != '\0')
    +	return grub_error (grub_errno,
    +			   N_("non-numeric or invalid keyfile offset `%s'"),
    +			   state[OPTION_KEYFILE_OFFSET].arg);
    +    }
    +
    +  if (state[OPTION_KEYFILE_SIZE].set) /* keyfile-size */
    +    {
    +	  grub_errno = GRUB_ERR_NONE;
    +	  keyfile_size = grub_strtoull (state[OPTION_KEYFILE_SIZE].arg, &p, 0);
    +
    +	  if (state[OPTION_KEYFILE_SIZE].arg[0] == '\0' || *p != '\0')
    +	    return grub_error (grub_errno,
    +			       N_("non-numeric or invalid keyfile size `%s'"),
    +			       state[OPTION_KEYFILE_SIZE].arg);
    +
    +	  if (keyfile_size == 0)
    +	    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE, N_("key file size is 0"));
    +
    +	  if (keyfile_size > GRUB_CRYPTODISK_MAX_KEYFILE_SIZE)
    +	    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE,
    +			       N_("key file size exceeds maximum (%d)"),
    +			       GRUB_CRYPTODISK_MAX_KEYFILE_SIZE);
    +    }
    +
    +  if (state[OPTION_KEYFILE].set)  /* key-file */
    +    file_ctx.keyfile = grub_strdup (state[OPTION_KEYFILE].arg);
    +
    +  if (file_ctx.keyfile == NULL)
    +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("no file specified for the file key protector"));
    +
    +  file_ctx.keyfile_offset = keyfile_offset;
    +  file_ctx.keyfile_size = keyfile_size;
    +
    +  /* This command only initializes the protector, so nothing else to do. */
    +
    +  return GRUB_ERR_NONE;
    +}
    +
    +static grub_err_t
    +file_clear_cmd_handler (grub_extcmd_context_t ctxt __attribute__ ((unused)),
    +			int argc, char **args __attribute__ ((unused)))
    +{
    +  if (argc > 0)
    +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("file_key_protector_clear accepts no arguments"));
    +
    +  grub_free ((void *) file_ctx.keyfile);
    +  grub_memset (&file_ctx, 0, sizeof (file_ctx));
    +
    +  return GRUB_ERR_NONE;
    +}

    +static struct grub_key_protector mfa_key_protector =
    +  {
    +    .name = "mfa",
    +    .recover_key = mfa_recover_key
    +  };
    +
    +static struct grub_key_protector password_key_protector =
    +  {
    +    .name = "password",
    +    .recover_key = password_recover_key
    +  };
    +
    +static struct grub_key_protector file_key_protector =
    +  {
    +    .name = "file",
    +    .recover_key = file_recover_key
    +  };
    +
    +GRUB_MOD_INIT (mfa)
    +{
    +  mfa_init_cmd =
    +    grub_register_extcmd ("mfa_key_protector_init",
    +			  mfa_init_cmd_handler, 0,
    +			  N_("[-1 protector] [-2 protector]"),
    +			  N_("Initialize the MFA key protector."),
    +			  mfa_init_cmd_options);
    +  mfa_clear_cmd =
    +    grub_register_extcmd ("mfa_key_protector_clear",
    +			  mfa_clear_cmd_handler, 0, NULL,
    +			  N_("Clear the MFA key protector if previously initialized."),
    +			  NULL);
    +
    +  password_init_cmd =
    +    grub_register_extcmd ("pw_key_protector_init",
    +			  password_init_cmd_handler, 0,
    +			  N_("[-p password]"),
    +			  N_("Initialize the password key protector."),
    +			  password_init_cmd_options);
    +  password_clear_cmd =
    +    grub_register_extcmd ("pw_key_protector_clear",
    +			  password_clear_cmd_handler, 0, NULL,
    +			  N_("Clear the password key protector if previously initialized."),
    +			  NULL);
    +
    +  file_init_cmd =
    +    grub_register_extcmd ("file_key_protector_init",
    +			  file_init_cmd_handler, 0,
    +			  N_("[-k path] [-O offset] [-S size]"),
    +			  N_("Initialize the file key protector."),
    +			  file_init_cmd_options);
    +  file_clear_cmd =
    +    grub_register_extcmd ("file_key_protector_clear",
    +			  file_clear_cmd_handler, 0, NULL,
    +			  N_("Clear the file key protector if previously initialized."),
    +			  NULL);
    +
    +  grub_key_protector_register (&mfa_key_protector);
    +  grub_key_protector_register (&password_key_protector);
    +  grub_key_protector_register (&file_key_protector);
    +}
    +
    +GRUB_MOD_FINI (mfa)
    +{
    +  grub_key_protector_unregister (&file_key_protector);
    +  grub_key_protector_unregister (&password_key_protector);
    +  grub_key_protector_unregister (&mfa_key_protector);
    +
    +  grub_unregister_extcmd (file_clear_cmd);
    +  grub_unregister_extcmd (file_init_cmd);
    +
    +  grub_unregister_extcmd (password_clear_cmd);
    +  grub_unregister_extcmd (password_init_cmd);
    +
    +  grub_unregister_extcmd (mfa_clear_cmd);
    +  grub_unregister_extcmd (mfa_init_cmd);

∴ Thinking…

  Now I've seen the full module. Let me analyze for issues:

  1. password_recover_key() double-return issue (lines 265-303):
    - Line 273-274: If password_ctx.password_len != 0, returns pointer to password_ctx.password
    - But this pointer is stored statically in password_ctx and should not be freed by the caller
    - The caller of grub_key_protector_recover_key() will likely free the returned key
    - This will cause a use-after-free when password_ctx.password is accessed again

  Looking at mfa_recover_key() lines 246-253:
  if (p1_key != NULL)
    {
      grub_memset (p1_key, 0, p1_size);
      grub_free (p1_key);
    }

  Yes, it frees the keys. So if password_recover_key() returns a pointer to the static password_ctx.password, and the MFA module frees
  it, then password_ctx.password becomes a dangling pointer.

  **This is a bug.**Actually, wait. Let me re-read more carefully. Looking at the password init handler (lines 478-497):

  if (state[OPTION_PASSWORD].set)  /* password */
    {
      password_ctx.password = (grub_uint8_t *) grub_strdup (state[OPTION_PASSWORD].arg);
      password_ctx.password_len = grub_strlen (state[OPTION_PASSWORD].arg);
    }

  So password_ctx.password is allocated with grub_strdup. If it's returned directly and then freed by the caller, that's OK. But then
  the clear handler (lines 499-510) will try to free it again:

  grub_free ((void *) password_ctx.password);
  grub_memset (&password_ctx, 0, sizeof (password_ctx));

  The flow creates a double-free vulnerability: initialization allocates the password via grub_strdup, recovery returns that pointer,
  the MFA module frees it, and then the clear handler attempts to free the same pointer again. This is a critical bug.

  The proper solution requires password_recover_key() to either allocate a fresh copy for the caller or null out the static reference
  after returning it. The intended logic appears to be returning a pre-set command-line password without prompting, or prompting the
  user if none exists. The fix should allocate new memory and copy the password data rather than returning the internal pointer
  directly.

  Scanning the remaining code, the file key protector and MFA protector implementations appear sound, with proper memory management and
   cleanup routines. The double-free vulnerability in the password recovery function stands out as the primary concern.

● Write(/home/lpcs/lpcsf-new/test/rhel/packages/grub2/grub-devel/reviews/2026-01-0039.md)
  ⎿  Wrote 6 lines to /home/lpcs/lpcsf-new/test/rhel/packages/grub2/grub-devel/reviews/2026-01-0039.md
     # AI Review: MR !69 - Add MFA key protector

     3 commits: Adds multi-factor authentication for encrypted partitions by combining keys from two protectors (password + file).

     - **Double-free bug** (grub-core/commands/mfa.c:271-274): `password_recover_key()` returns pointer to static
     `password_ctx.password` when password is pre-set. Caller (`mfa_recover_key()` at line 246-253) frees this pointer. Later,
     `password_clear_cmd_handler()` (line 508) attempts to free the same pointer again, causing double-free. Should allocate and
     copy password data instead of returning static pointer directly.

● Bash(git show 384f82558)
  ⎿  commit 384f825589a8d20260629f62fb208193779e673b
     Author: Avnish Chouhan <avnish@linux.ibm.com>
     Date:   Tue Jan 27 19:18:14 2026 +0530

         Mandatory install device check for PowerPC

         This patch adds a check on install_device while installing grub for PowerPC.
         If install_device is not mentioned in grub2-install and machine is detected
         as PowerPC, the error will be thrown and it will terminates the grub2-install
         operation. Running grub2-install on PowerPC without the install_device may
         result in bootlist corruption. When no install device is specified, it attempts
         to load images from the filesystem, which leads to nvram bootlist corruption.
         The idea is to fail the operation and avoid creating the invalid boot entry.

         Signed-off-by: Avnish Chouhan <avnish@linux.ibm.com>

     diff --git a/util/grub-install.c b/util/grub-install.c
     index 060246589..896ea822a 100644
     --- a/util/grub-install.c
     +++ b/util/grub-install.c
     @@ -1289,6 +1289,19 @@ main (int argc, char *argv[])
      	      is_prep = 0;
      	    }
      	}
     +#if defined(__powerpc__)
     +     else
     +       {
     +         /*
     +	   * As the machine has been detected as PowerPC and not a PowerMac. We need to check
     +	   * whether the install_device has been mentioned while installing. If no device has been
     +	   * mentioned, we need to exit and mark it as an error as the install_device is required for
     +	   * PowerPC installation. An installation with no device mentioned may lead to corruptions.
     +	   */
     +          if (!install_device)
     +            grub_util_error ("%s", _("install device isn't specified, required for PowerPC"));
     +       }
     +#endif /* __powerpc__ */
          }

        size_t ndev = 0;

∴ Thinking…

  This adds a check for PowerPC to require an install device. The logic looks correct - if no install_device is specified on PowerPC
  (and it's not PowerMac), it throws an error to prevent bootlist corruption.

  Let me check if there are any issues with this implementation...

  Looking at the code structure:
  - Line 1292-1293: #if defined(__powerpc__) - platform guard
  - Line 1294: else - this corresponds to some if statement above in the original code
  - Line 1302: Check if !install_device and throw error

  The code looks correct. The error prevents corruption by failing early.

         efi: Always close protocols after usage

         firmware resource leak.

● Bash(git show f42c040ed)
  ⎿ commit f42c040eddd3ec1e2610c2311bec89bc696de45c
    Author: Christian Schoenebeck <linux_oss@crudebyte.com>
    Date:   Mon Feb 9 19:56:00 2026 +0100

        zfs: fix root dataset detection on encrypted pools

        grub-probe fails on encrypted zfs pools, causing $rpool variable
        to be empty. Therefore check variables $rpool and $bootfs to
        be both non-empty strings before assembling LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE.

        If one of the two variables is empty, use "zfs list" as fallback
        for detecting the zfs root dataset instead.

        Signed-off-by: Christian Schoenebeck <linux_oss@crudebyte.com>

    diff --git a/util/grub.d/10_linux.in b/util/grub.d/10_linux.in
    index 1233b112b..0665ed282 100644
    --- a/util/grub.d/10_linux.in
    +++ b/util/grub.d/10_linux.in
    @@ -75,7 +75,13 @@ case x"$GRUB_FS" in
         xzfs)
     	rpool=`${grub_probe} --device ${GRUB_DEVICE} --target=fs_label 2>/dev/null || zdb -l ${GRUB_DEVICE} | awk -F \' '/ name/ {
    print $2 }'`
     	bootfs="`make_system_path_relative_to_its_root / | sed -e "s,@$,,"`"
    -	LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE="ZFS=${rpool}${bootfs%/}"
    +	# grub-probe fails on encrypted zfs pools, use zfs list as fallback
    +	if [ -n "$rpool" ] && [ -n "$bootfs" ]; then
    +	    LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE="ZFS=${rpool}${bootfs%/}"
    +	else
    +	    root_dataset=`zfs list -Ho mountpoint,name | grep '^/[[:space:]]' | tail -n1 | cut -f 2 2>/dev/null || true`
    +	    LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE="ZFS=${root_dataset}"
    +	fi
     	;;
     esac

∴ Thinking…

  Let me analyze this ZFS fix carefully:1. Original code: LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE="ZFS=${rpool}${bootfs%/}"
  2. New code: Checks if $rpool and $bootfs are non-empty before using them3. Fallback: Uses zfs list if either variable is empty

  The logic looks good. The fallback uses:
  root_dataset=`zfs list -Ho mountpoint,name | grep '^/[[:space:]]' | tail -n1 | cut -f 2 2>/dev/null || true`

  This finds the dataset mounted at /. The || true prevents the script from failing if the command fails.Is there any issue? Let me
  think...

  - What if root_dataset is also empty after the fallback? Then LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE="ZFS=" which would be invalid. But this is probably
  acceptable - the boot will fail with a clear error rather than silently using wrong values.
  - The regex '^/[[:space:]]' looks for mountpoint starting with / followed by whitespace. This is correct for zfs list output format.
  - The tail -n1 takes the last matching line. If there are multiple datasets mounted at /, this might not be correct. But typically
  there's only one root dataset.Overall, this looks like a reasonable fix for the encrypted pool issue. I don't see any bugs.

         efi: Drop support for UGA Draw and Console Control protocols

         Let's stop GRUB from becoming a software museum and drop support for
         deprecated EFI protocols.

         - UGA Draw protocol was deprecated in UEFI 2.0 in 2006.
         - Console Control protocol was an implementation detail of
           the deprecated Intel EDK (not EDK II).

     +static int
     +forward_word (struct screen *screen, int update)
     +{
     +  int prev_column = screen->column;
     +  struct line *linep;
     +
     +  linep = screen->lines + screen->line;
     +  while (screen->column < linep->len && grub_isspace (linep->buf[screen->column]))
     +    ++screen->column;
     +  while (screen->column < linep->len && grub_isalnum (linep->buf[screen->column]))
     +    ++screen->column;
     +  if (prev_column == screen->column)
     +    {
     +      if (! forward_char (screen, 0))
     +	return 0;
     +    }
     +  if (update)
     +    update_screen_all (screen, screen->num_lines, 0, 0, 0, NO_LINE);
     +  return 1;
     +}
     +
     +static int
     +backward_word (struct screen *screen, int update)
     +{
     +  int prev_column = screen->column;
     +  struct line *linep;
     +
     +  linep = screen->lines + screen->line;
     +  while (screen->column > 0 && grub_isspace (linep->buf[screen->column - 1]))
     +    screen->column--;
     +  while (screen->column > 0 && grub_isalnum (linep->buf[screen->column - 1]))
     +    screen->column--;
     +  if (prev_column == screen->column)
     +    {
     +      if (! backward_char (screen, 0))
     +	return 0;
     +    }
     +  if (update)
     +    update_screen_all (screen, screen->num_lines, 0, 0, 0, NO_LINE);
     +  return 1;
     +}
     +
      static int
      previous_line (struct screen *screen, int update)
      {
     @@ -869,6 +911,88 @@ backward_delete_char (struct screen *screen, int update)
        return 1;
      }

     +static int
     +delete_word (struct screen *screen, int swap_whitespace, int update)
     +{
     +  struct line *linep;
     +  unsigned i;
     +  int del;
     +
     +  linep = screen->lines + screen->line;
     +  for (i = 0; i < screen->nterms; i++)
     +    screen->terms[i].orig_num =
     +      get_logical_num_lines (linep, &screen->terms[i]);
     +
     +  del = 0;
     +  if (swap_whitespace)
     +    {
     +      while (screen->column + del < linep->len
     +	     && grub_isalnum (linep->buf[screen->column + del]))
     +	del++;
     +      while (screen->column + del < linep->len
     +	     && grub_isspace (linep->buf[screen->column + del]))
     +	del++;
     +    }
     +  else
     +    {
     +      while (screen->column + del < linep->len
     +	     && grub_isspace (linep->buf[screen->column + del]))
     +	del++;
     +      while (screen->column + del < linep->len
     +	     && grub_isalnum (linep->buf[screen->column + del]))
     +	del++;
     +    }
     +
     +  if (del == 0)
     +    {
     +      if (!delete_char (screen, update))
     +	return 0;
     +    }
     +  else
     +    {
     +      grub_memmove (linep->buf + screen->column,
     +		    linep->buf + screen->column + del,
     +		    (linep->len - screen->column -
     +		     del) * sizeof (linep->buf[0]));
     +      linep->len -= del;
     +
     +      for (i = 0; i < screen->nterms; i++)
     +	{
     +	  grub_free (linep->pos[i]);
     +	  linep->pos[i] = 0;
     +	}
     +
     +      screen->real_column = screen->column;
     +
     +      if (update)
     +	{
     +	  for (i = 0; i < screen->nterms; i++)
     +	    {
     +	      int new_num;
     +	      new_num = get_logical_num_lines (linep, &screen->terms[i]);
     +	      if (screen->terms[i].orig_num != new_num)
     +		update_screen (screen, &screen->terms[i],
     +			       screen->line, screen->column, 0, 0, ALL_LINES);
     +	      else
     +		update_screen (screen, &screen->terms[i],
     +			       screen->line, screen->column, 0, 0,
     +			       SINGLE_LINE);
     +	    }
     +	}
     +    }
     +  return 1;
     +}
     +
     +static int
     +backward_delete_word (struct screen *screen, int update)
     +{
     +  if (screen->line == 0 && screen->column == 0)
     +    return 1;
     +  if (!backward_word (screen, 0))
     +    return 0;
     +  return delete_word (screen, 1, update);
     +}
     +

      kill_line (struct screen *screen, int continuous, int update)
      {
     @@ -1436,6 +1560,27 @@ grub_menu_entry_run (grub_menu_entry_t entry)

  Let me analyze this code for bugs:1. forward_word() (lines 689-708):   - Skips whitespace, then skips alphanumeric characters
  - If no movement, calls forward_char() as fallback
  - Looks correct2. backward_word() (lines 710-729):   - Skips whitespace backwards, then alphanumeric backwards
  - Uses screen->column - 1 for indexing, which is correct
  - Looks correct

  3. delete_word() (lines 914-984):   - Has a swap_whitespace parameter to control deletion order   - Uses grub_memmove() to shift
  buffer content
    - Frees and resets linep->pos[i] for all terminals   - Looks correct4. backward_delete_word() (lines 986-994):   - Moves back by
  one word, then deletes   - Returns 1 early if already at start (line 0, column 0)
    - Looks correctAll the implementations look solid. The logic is clear, error handling is present, and memory management looks

● Bash(git show b90405a81 --stat)
  ⎿  commit b90405a81759dcde0e883471e529e20a161eb86f
     Author: Mate Kukri <mate.kukri@canonical.com>
     Date:   Wed Feb 11 10:21:00 2026 +0000

         normal/cmdline: Add support for jump/delete word shortcuts

         These work similarly to bash/readline. Word here means a possible run of
         whitespace plus a run of alphanumeric characters (viewed from the approach
         direction relative to the cursor).

         Ctrl+left/right: jump word left or right
         Ctrl+delete: delete word right of the cursor
         Ctrl+w, Alt+backspace: delete word left of the cursor

         Signed-off-by: Mate Kukri <mate.kukri@canonical.com>

      grub-core/normal/cmdline.c | 60 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
      1 file changed, 60 insertions(+)

∴ Thinking…

     Author: Mate Kukri <mate.kukri@canonical.com>
     Date:   Wed Feb 11 10:21:00 2026 +0000

         normal/cmdline: Add support for jump/delete word shortcuts

         These work similarly to bash/readline. Word here means a possible run of
         whitespace plus a run of alphanumeric characters (viewed from the approach
         direction relative to the cursor).

         Ctrl+left/right: jump word left or right
         Ctrl+delete: delete word right of the cursor
         Ctrl+w, Alt+backspace: delete word left of the cursor

         Signed-off-by: Mate Kukri <mate.kukri@canonical.com>

     diff --git a/grub-core/normal/cmdline.c b/grub-core/normal/cmdline.c
     index 0875250be..39ee6ea16 100644
     --- a/grub-core/normal/cmdline.c
     +++ b/grub-core/normal/cmdline.c
     @@ -355,6 +355,49 @@ cl_insert (struct cmdline_term *cl_terms, unsigned nterms,
          }
      }

     +static void
     +skip_word_rev (struct cmdline_term *cl_terms, unsigned nterms,
     +	       grub_uint32_t *buf, grub_size_t *lpos,
     +	       grub_size_t *llen, int delete)
     +{
     +  grub_size_t endpos = *lpos;
     +  /* trailing whitespace sequence */
     +  while (*lpos > 0 && grub_isspace(buf[*lpos - 1]))
     +    --*lpos;
     +  /* alphanumeric sequence */
     +  while (*lpos > 0 && grub_isalnum(buf[*lpos - 1]))
     +    --*lpos;
     +  /* skip one char if the above logic yielded nothing and we can */
     +  if (*lpos > 0 && endpos - *lpos == 0)
     +    --*lpos;
     +  cl_set_pos_all (cl_terms, nterms, *lpos);
     +  if (delete)
     +    cl_delete (cl_terms, nterms, buf, *lpos, llen, endpos - *lpos);
     +}
     +
     +static void
     +skip_word_fwd (struct cmdline_term *cl_terms, unsigned nterms,
     +	       grub_uint32_t *buf, grub_size_t *lpos,
     +	       grub_size_t *llen, int delete)
     +{
     +  grub_size_t endpos = *lpos;
     +  /* leading whitespace sequence */
     +  while (endpos < *llen && grub_isspace(buf[endpos]))
     +    ++endpos;
     +  /* alphanumeric sequence */
     +  while (endpos < *llen && grub_isalnum(buf[endpos]))
     +    ++endpos;
     +  /* skip one char if the above logic yielded nothing and we can */
     +  if (endpos < *llen && endpos - *lpos == 0)
     +    ++endpos;
     +  if (delete)
     +    cl_delete (cl_terms, nterms, buf, *lpos, llen, endpos - *lpos);
     +  else
     +    {
     +      *lpos = endpos;
     +      cl_set_pos_all (cl_terms, nterms, *lpos);
     +    }
     +}

      /* Get a command-line. If ESC is pushed, return zero,
         otherwise return command line.  */
     @@ -648,6 +691,23 @@ grub_cmdline_get (const char *prompt_translated)
      	    cl_insert (cl_terms, nterms, &lpos, &llen, &max_len, &buf, kill_buf);
      	  break;

     +	case GRUB_TERM_CTRL | GRUB_TERM_KEY_LEFT:
     +	  skip_word_rev (cl_terms, nterms, buf, &lpos, &llen, 0);
     +	  break;
     +
     +	case GRUB_TERM_CTRL | GRUB_TERM_KEY_RIGHT:
     +	  skip_word_fwd (cl_terms, nterms, buf, &lpos, &llen, 0);
     +	  break;
     +
     +	case GRUB_TERM_CTRL | 'w':
     +	case GRUB_TERM_ALT | GRUB_TERM_BACKSPACE:
     +	  skip_word_rev (cl_terms, nterms, buf, &lpos, &llen, 1);
     +	  break;
     +
     +	case GRUB_TERM_CTRL | GRUB_TERM_KEY_DC:
     +	  skip_word_fwd (cl_terms, nterms, buf, &lpos, &llen, 1);
     +	  break;
     +
      	case GRUB_TERM_ESC:
      	  grub_free (cl_terms);
      	  grub_free (buf);

∴ Thinking…

  Let me analyze this code:1. skip_word_rev() (lines 358-376):   - Skips trailing whitespace backwards   - Skips alphanumeric backwards
  - If no movement, skips one char as fallback   - Optionally deletes   - Looks correct2. skip_word_fwd() (lines 378-399):   - Skips
  leading whitespace   - Skips alphanumeric   - If no movement, skips one char as fallback
  - Optionally deletes or moves cursor   - Looks correctThe implementations are similar to !76 but for the command line editor. The
  logic is sound and consistent.

● Bash(git show 7bced2906)
  ⎿  commit 7bced290691c1a63310b2fb20b9b452c92b9d355
     Author: Mikhail Lappo <miklelappo@gmail.com>
     Date:   Wed Feb 25 14:06:57 2026 +0100

         loader/efi/fdt: Ensure FDT blob is 8-byte aligned

         libfdt requires the FDT blob to be 8-byte aligned. The alignment is
         validated in fdt_check_header(), and misaligned blobs may be rejected
         or cause failures on architectures that enforce strict alignment.

         Currently the blob is allocated with grub_malloc(), which does not
         guarantee 8-byte alignment. Replace it with grub_memalign(8, size)
         to ensure the required alignment.

         Signed-off-by: Mikhail Lappo <miklelappo@gmail.com>

     diff --git a/grub-core/loader/efi/fdt.c b/grub-core/loader/efi/fdt.c
     index e510b3491..f25d362d3 100644
     --- a/grub-core/loader/efi/fdt.c
     +++ b/grub-core/loader/efi/fdt.c
     @@ -38,6 +38,7 @@ static void *fdt;
      #define FDT_ADDR_SIZE_EXTRA ((2 * grub_fdt_prop_entry_size (sizeof(grub_uint32_t))) + \
                                   sizeof (FDT_ADDR_CELLS_STRING) + \
                                   sizeof (FDT_SIZE_CELLS_STRING))
     +#define FDT_BLOB_ALIGNMENT 8

      static const struct grub_arg_option options_fdtdump[] = {
        {"prop",	'p', 0, N_("Get property."), N_("prop"), ARG_TYPE_STRING},
     @@ -145,7 +146,7 @@ grub_cmd_devicetree (grub_command_t cmd __attribute__ ((unused)),
          goto out;

        size = grub_file_size (dtb);
     -  blob = grub_malloc (size);
     +  blob = grub_memalign (FDT_BLOB_ALIGNMENT, size);
        if (!blob)
          goto out;

∴ Thinking…

  This is a straightforward alignment fix. Changes grub_malloc() to grub_memalign(8, size) to ensure 8-byte alignment required by
  libfdt. This is correct.

  Wait, let me check if grub_free() can handle memory allocated with grub_memalign(). In GRUB, both use the same underlying allocator,
  so grub_free() should work fine with grub_memalign() allocated memory. This is a proper fix with no issues.

∴ Thinking…

  I can see that:
  1. Line 100 in the actual file (within the loop at line 103-111): variable_name_string = grub_calloc (...) - this IS allocated in the
   loop
  2. Line 99 (after realloc): variable_name = tmp; - after this, the code falls through to line 100 which allocates
  variable_name_string - so this processes data even after realloc. It should continue.
  3. After line 99, there's no continue statement, so it falls through to process the (potentially incomplete) data from the failed
  get_next_variable_name call.

  Let me see the cleanup section to verify the memory leak claim.

● Bash(git show e2f2470dd grub-core/commands/efi/lsefivar.c | tail -30)

     +static grub_err_t
     +grub_cmd_lsefivar (grub_extcmd_context_t ctxt,
     +                int argc __attribute__((unused)), char **argv __attribute__((unused)))
     +{

     +               return dump_efi_variables (true); /* true: dump variables and contents*/

     +}

∴ Thinking…

  I can see:
  - Line 113 (in the actual diff): grub_free (variable_name_string); - this frees it only once after the loop
  - But variable_name_string is allocated in EACH iteration of the loop (line 100)
  - So each iteration leaks the previous allocation

  Let me verify all 3 claims:
  1. Memory leak (line 100): YES - variable_name_string allocated each iteration, freed only once
  2. Missing continue (line 88-99): YES - after realloc on BUFFER_TOO_SMALL, should continue to retry get_next_variable_name, but
  instead falls through
  3. Missing error handling (line 85): YES - if status is neither NOT_FOUND nor BUFFER_TOO_SMALL (e.g., some other error), code
  continues to line 100 and processes invalid data

  All claims verified. MR !59 review is correct. Moving to !60.

        From 1e062220d64ce1cf491ad670f368c06072668d2b Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
        From: Shreenidhi Shedi <shreenidhi.shedi@broadcom.com>
        Date: Wed, 14 May 2025 14:47:15 +0530
        Subject: [PATCH v3 1/1] cmdline: fix command line parsing in grub

        Previously, the command line construction function
        (grub_create_loader_cmdline) escaped single and double quotes, which is
        unnecessary and potentially problematic since the kernel command line
        handler does not support escaped quotes. This patch removes the escaping
        of these characters, ensuring that the constructed command line is
        passed to the kernel as intended.

        For example:

        In /boot/grub/grub.cfg
        ...
        set user_cmdline="dyndbg='+p; func smp_callin -p'"
        ...
        menuentry "Photon" {
    Switched to branch '2025-07-0295'

  issues found" which is appropriate for this change. The commit message clearly explains the reasoning. Moving to !61.

● Bash(git checkout 2025-09-0165 && git show bb1396681 grub-core/osdep/unix/getroot.c | grep -A8 -B3 "Skip mount point")
  ⎿  Your branch is up to date with 'pvalena/2025-09-0165'.
     Author: Michael Chang <mchang@suse.com>
     Date:   Tue Sep 16 14:38:05 2025 +0800

         getroot: Skip mount points in grub_find_device

         The grub_find_device function scans a starting directory, typically
         /dev, for device files with matching major and minor numbers. During
         this process, it recursively descends into subdirectories.

         However, this can significantly slow down the scan if a subdirectory is
         a mount point not related to devtmpfs, especially if it contains a large
         number of files.
     --
      	  /* Find it recursively.  */
      	  char *res;

     +	  /* Skip mount point */
     +	  if (st.st_dev != st_dir.st_dev)
     +	    {
     +	      grub_util_info ("skip mount point %s/%s", dir, ent->d_name);
     +	      continue;
     +	    }
     +
      	  res = grub_find_device (ent->d_name, dev);
     Switched to branch '2025-09-0165'

∴ Thinking…

  MR !61 is correct - it skips mount points by comparing st.st_dev != st_dir.st_dev. The review correctly states this is an
  optimization with no issues. Moving to !62.

         Define a Containerfile and instructions to build distro images

         Containers bring the ability to have ready-to-use environments
         isolated completely from the build environment. Once users manually
         build it, they can launch the desired container, jump into it and have
         a setup ready for development. On the other hand, if users prefer to
         use bare metal instead of a containerized environment, it is still
         useful to have a the full list of the required packages.

         Signed-off-by: Leo Sandoval <lsandova@redhat.com>

● Bash(git checkout 2025-09-0265 && git show fbaaf8ba0 grub-core/Makefile.core.def | grep -B3 -A8 "video_bochs\|video_cirrus")
  ⎿  Your branch is up to date with 'pvalena/2025-09-0265'.
     Author: Andrew Hamilton <adhamilt@gmail.com>
     Date:   Mon Sep 29 21:23:40 2025 -0500

         build: Disable building video_bochs/cirrus for EFI x86

         Disable building video_bochs and video_cirrus for EFI x86 platforms (i386_efi
         and x86_64_efi). For x86 EFI platforms efi_gop or efi_uga should provide the
         video support. Currently, in EFI platforms there is a risk that both the
         legacy device is present and the EFI video interface - which can cause
         unexpected video corruption when a mix of the two interfaces are used. This
         can happen when all_video is loaded for simplicity in a GRUB config.

         Fixes: https://savannah.gnu.org/bugs/index.php?66200

     --
     +++ b/grub-core/Makefile.core.def
     @@ -2194,13 +2194,21 @@ module = {
      module = {
        name = video_cirrus;
        x86 = video/cirrus.c;
     -  enable = x86;
     +  enable = i386_pc;
     +  enable = i386_qemu;
     +  enable = i386_coreboot;
     +  enable = i386_multiboot;
     +  enable = i386_ieee1275;
      };

      module = {
        name = video_bochs;
        x86 = video/bochs.c;
     -  enable = x86;
     +  enable = i386_pc;
     +  enable = i386_qemu;
     +  enable = i386_coreboot;
     +  enable = i386_multiboot;
     +  enable = i386_ieee1275;
      };

     # AI Review: MR !47 - Add submenu --menutitle option

     Adds --menutitle option to submenu command for setting menu title independently.

     - **Critical bug** (grub-core/commands/menuentry.c:269-270): Double increment in loop causes wrong buffer allocation. Loop
iterator `t++` and loop body `*t++` both increment pointer, skipping every other character:
     ```c
     for (t = title; *t; t++)
       len += (*t++ == '\'' ? 4 : 1);  // DOUBLE INCREMENT
     ```
     ```

   - **reviews/2025-07-0196.md** (MR !59 - MEMORY LEAK + LOGIC ERRORS)
     ```markdown
     - **Memory leak** (line 100): `variable_name_string` allocated in loop but only freed once at line 113. Each iteration leaks
the previous allocation.

     - **Missing continue after realloc** (line 88-99): When `GRUB_EFI_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL`, code reallocates buffer but falls through
 to process invalid data.

     - **Missing error handling** (line 85): If `get_next_variable_name()` returns error other than `NOT_FOUND` or
`BUFFER_TOO_SMALL`, code still processes invalid data.
     ```

   - **reviews/2025-12-0030.md** (MR !68 - DOUBLE-FREE)
     ```markdown
     - **Double-free risk** (grub-core/commands/blsuki.c:1380,1407 in commit 1): Code calls `grub_free(esp_disk->partition)` then
later `grub_disk_close(esp_disk)`. The close function will attempt to free the partition again, causing double-free.
     ```

   - **reviews/2026-01-0039.md** (MR !69 - DOUBLE-FREE)
     ```markdown
     - **Double-free bug** (grub-core/commands/mfa.c:271-274): `password_recover_key()` returns pointer to static
`password_ctx.password` when password is pre-set. Caller (`mfa_recover_key()` at line 246-253) frees this pointer. Later,
`password_clear_cmd_handler()` (line 508) attempts to free the same pointer again, causing double-free.
     ```

● Bash(git show fbaaf8ba0 grub-core/Makefile.core.def | grep -B3 -A8 "video_bochs\|video_cirrus")
  ⎿  Author: Andrew Hamilton <adhamilt@gmail.com>
     Date:   Mon Sep 29 21:23:40 2025 -0500

         build: Disable building video_bochs/cirrus for EFI x86

         Disable building video_bochs and video_cirrus for EFI x86 platforms (i386_efi
         and x86_64_efi). For x86 EFI platforms efi_gop or efi_uga should provide the
         video support. Currently, in EFI platforms there is a risk that both the
         legacy device is present and the EFI video interface - which can cause
         unexpected video corruption when a mix of the two interfaces are used. This
         can happen when all_video is loaded for simplicity in a GRUB config.

         Fixes: https://savannah.gnu.org/bugs/index.php?66200

     --
     +++ b/grub-core/Makefile.core.def
     @@ -2194,13 +2194,21 @@ module = {
      module = {
        name = video_cirrus;
        x86 = video/cirrus.c;
     -  enable = x86;
     +  enable = i386_pc;
     +  enable = i386_qemu;
     +  enable = i386_coreboot;
     +  enable = i386_multiboot;
     +  enable = i386_ieee1275;
      };

      module = {
        name = video_bochs;
        x86 = video/bochs.c;
     -  enable = x86;
     +  enable = i386_pc;
     +  enable = i386_qemu;
     +  enable = i386_coreboot;
     +  enable = i386_multiboot;
     +  enable = i386_ieee1275;
      };

● Bash(git show 404061969 --stat)
  ⎿  commit 404061969e47875240f15ab1f143aa59a822d1d6
     Author: Renaud Métrich <rmetrich@redhat.com>
     Date:   Wed Nov 26 15:34:58 2025 +0100

         efi: new 'connectefi' command

         When network booting is used, trying to chainload to the local disk
         (which is used in deployment tools such as Red Hat Satellite) may fail
         when searching for the boot loader, e.g. /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi:
         the boot loader file is listed, but not readable, because UEFI DISK I/O
         and/or SCSI DISK I/O devices are not connected.
         Sometimes devices are connected but not the partition of the disk
         matching $prefix, causing partition to not be found by"chainloader".

         This issue was seen already on:
         - VMWare systems with efi.quickboot enabled (which is the default)
         - QEMU/KVM when network booting and not enabling any disk
         - Physical hardware such as Cisco and HPE systems

         Typical chainload configuration used:
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------
         unset prefix
         search --file --set=prefix /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         if [ -n "$prefix" ]; then
           chainloader ($prefix)/EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         ...
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------

         This patch introduces a new "connectefi pciroot|disk|all" command which
         recursively connects all EFI devices starting from a given controller
         type:
         - if 'pciroot' is specified, recursion is performed for all PCI root
           handles
         - if 'disk' is specified, recursion is performed for all SCSI and DISK
           I/O handles (recommended usage to avoid connecting unwanted handles
           which may impact Grub performances)
         - if 'all' is specified, recursion is performed on all handles (not
           recommended since it may heavily impact Grub performances)

         Typical grub.cfg snippet would then be:
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------
         connectefi disk
         unset prefix
         search --file --set=prefix /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         if [ -n "$prefix" ]; then
           chainloader ($prefix)/EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         ...
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------

         The code is easily extensible to handle other arguments in the future if
         needed.

         Signed-off-by: Renaud Métrich <rmetrich@redhat.com>

      docs/grub.texi                      |  33 ++++++
      grub-core/Makefile.core.def         |   6 +
      grub-core/commands/efi/connectefi.c | 227 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
      grub-core/disk/efi/efidisk.c        |  13 +++
      include/grub/efi/disk.h             |   2 +
      5 files changed, 281 insertions(+)

∴ Thinking…

     +typedef enum search_item_flag search_item_flag_t;
     +
     +struct search_item
     +{
     +  grub_guid_t guid;

     +typedef struct search_item search_item_t;

     +static bool
     +is_in_list (grub_efi_handle_t handle, grub_efi_handle_list_t *handles)
     +{
     +  grub_efi_handle_list_t *e;
     +
     +  FOR_LIST_ELEMENTS (e, handles)
     +    if (e->handle == handle)
     +      return true;
     +
     +  return false;
     +}
     +
     +static void
     +free_handle_list (grub_efi_handle_list_t **handles_p)
     +{
     +  grub_efi_handle_list_t *e, *n;
     +
     +  FOR_LIST_ELEMENTS_SAFE (e, n, *handles_p)
     +    grub_free (e);
     +
     +  *handles_p = NULL;
     +}
     +
     +static grub_err_t
     +grub_cmd_connectefi (grub_command_t cmd __attribute__ ((unused)),
     +		     int argc, char **args)
     +{

     +    {
     +      { GRUB_EFI_PCI_ROOT_IO_GUID, "PCI root", SEARCH_RECURSIVE }
     +    };
     +  search_item_t disk_items[] =
     +    {
     +      { GRUB_EFI_PCI_ROOT_IO_GUID, "PCI root", SEARCH_NONE },
     +      { GRUB_EFI_PCI_IO_GUID, "PCI", SEARCH_LOOP },
     +      { GRUB_EFI_SCSI_IO_PROTOCOL_GUID, "SCSI I/O", SEARCH_RECURSIVE },
     +      { GRUB_EFI_DISK_IO_GUID, "DISK I/O", SEARCH_RECURSIVE }
     +    };
     +  search_item_t *items = NULL;
     +  int i, nitems = 0;
     +  grub_err_t err = GRUB_ERR_NONE;
     +  bool reenumerate_disks = false;
     +  grub_efi_handle_list_t *already_handled = NULL;
     +  grub_efi_handle_t *handles = NULL;
     +
     +  if (argc != 1)
     +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("one argument expected"));
     +
     +  if (grub_strcmp (args[0], "pciroot") == 0)
     +    {
     +      items = pciroot_items;
     +      nitems = ARRAY_SIZE (pciroot_items);
     +    }
     +  else if (grub_strcmp (args[0], "disk") == 0)
     +    {
     +      items = disk_items;
     +      nitems = ARRAY_SIZE (disk_items);
     +    }
     +  else if (grub_strcmp (args[0], "all") == 0)
     +    {
     +      items = NULL;
     +      nitems = 1;
     +    }
     +  else
     +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT,

     +      bool continue_search;
     +
     +      do

     +	  continue_search = false;

     +	  if (items != NULL)
     +	    {
     +	      if (++loop >= max_loop_depth)
     +		{
     +		  err = grub_error (GRUB_ERR_RECURSION_DEPTH,
     +			"connectefi: Maximum recursion depth exceeded for item '%s'",
     +			items[i].name);
     +		  goto fail;

     +					      NULL, NULL, &num_handles);

     +	  if (handles == NULL)
     +	    break; /* proceed to next item */
     +
     +	  for (j = 0; j < num_handles; j++)
     +	    {
     +	      grub_efi_handle_t handle = handles[j];
     +	      grub_efi_status_t status;
     +	      grub_efi_handle_list_t *item;
     +
     +	      /* Skip already handled handles */
     +	      if (is_in_list (handle, already_handled))
     +		{
     +		  grub_dprintf ("efi", "  handle %p: already processed\n", handle);
     +		  continue;
     +		}

     +			handle, NULL, NULL,

● Bash(git show 404061969 grub-core/commands/efi/connectefi.c | tail -80)
  ⎿  +					      NULL, NULL, &num_handles);

     +	    break; /* proceed to next item */

     +		  grub_dprintf ("efi", "  handle %p: already processed\n", handle);
     +		  continue;
     +		}
     +

     +			handle, NULL, NULL,
     +			(items == NULL) || items[i].flags & SEARCH_RECURSIVE ? 1 : 0);

     +		  reenumerate_disks = true;
     +		  grub_dprintf ("efi", "  handle %p: connected\n", handle);
     +		}
     +	      else
     +		grub_dprintf ("efi", "  handle %p: failed to connect (%"
     +			      PRIxGRUB_EFI_UINTN_T ")\n", handle, status);
     +
     +	      item = grub_malloc (sizeof (*item));
     +	      if (item == NULL)
     +		{
     +		  err = grub_errno;
     +		  goto fail; /* fatal */
     +		}
     +	      grub_list_push (GRUB_AS_LIST_P (&already_handled), GRUB_AS_LIST (item));
     +	      item->handle = handle;
     +	    }
     +
     +	  grub_free (handles);
     +	  handles = NULL;
     +	}
     +      while (continue_search == true);
     +
     +      /* We are done with this item, proceed to next one */
     +      free_handle_list (&already_handled);
     +    }
     +
     + fail:
     +  grub_free (handles);
     +  free_handle_list (&already_handled);
     +
     +  if (reenumerate_disks)
     +    grub_efidisk_reenumerate_disks ();
     +
     +  return err;
     +}

     +GRUB_MOD_INIT(connectefi)
     +{
