     Author: Leo Sandoval <lsandova@redhat.com>
     Date:   Fri Sep 19 17:39:18 2025 -0600

         Define a Containerfile and instructions to build distro images

         Containers bring the ability to have ready-to-use environments
         isolated completely from the build environment. Once users manually
         build it, they can launch the desired container, jump into it and have
         a setup ready for development. On the other hand, if users prefer to
         use bare metal instead of a containerized environment, it is still
         useful to have a the full list of the required packages.

         Signed-off-by: Leo Sandoval <lsandova@redhat.com>

● Read(file_path: "/home/lpcs/lpcsf-new/test/rhel/packages/grub2/grub-devel/reviews/2025-09-0265.md")

● Bash(git checkout 2025-09-0265 && git show fbaaf8ba0 grub-core/Makefile.core.def | grep -B3 -A8 "video_bochs\|video_cirrus")
  ⎿  Your branch is up to date with 'pvalena/2025-09-0265'.
     Author: Andrew Hamilton <adhamilt@gmail.com>
     Date:   Mon Sep 29 21:23:40 2025 -0500

         build: Disable building video_bochs/cirrus for EFI x86

         Disable building video_bochs and video_cirrus for EFI x86 platforms (i386_efi
         and x86_64_efi). For x86 EFI platforms efi_gop or efi_uga should provide the
         video support. Currently, in EFI platforms there is a risk that both the
         legacy device is present and the EFI video interface - which can cause
         unexpected video corruption when a mix of the two interfaces are used. This
         can happen when all_video is loaded for simplicity in a GRUB config.

         Fixes: https://savannah.gnu.org/bugs/index.php?66200

     --
     +++ b/grub-core/Makefile.core.def
     @@ -2194,13 +2194,21 @@ module = {
      module = {
        name = video_cirrus;
        x86 = video/cirrus.c;
     -  enable = x86;
     +  enable = i386_pc;
     +  enable = i386_qemu;
     +  enable = i386_coreboot;

     - **Memory leak** (line 100): `variable_name_string` allocated in loop but only freed once at line 113. Each iteration leaks the previous allocation.

     - **Missing continue after realloc** (line 88-99): When `GRUB_EFI_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL`, code reallocates buffer but falls through to process invalid
data.

     - **Missing error handling** (line 85): If `get_next_variable_name()` returns error other than `NOT_FOUND` or `BUFFER_TOO_SMALL`, code still processes
 invalid data.
     ```

   - **reviews/2025-12-0030.md** (MR !68 - DOUBLE-FREE)
     ```markdown
     - **Double-free risk** (grub-core/commands/blsuki.c:1380,1407 in commit 1): Code calls `grub_free(esp_disk->partition)` then later
`grub_disk_close(esp_disk)`. The close function will attempt to free the partition again, causing double-free.
     ```

   - **reviews/2026-01-0039.md** (MR !69 - DOUBLE-FREE)
     ```markdown
     - **Double-free bug** (grub-core/commands/mfa.c:271-274): `password_recover_key()` returns pointer to static `password_ctx.password` when password is
pre-set. Caller (`mfa_recover_key()` at line 246-253) frees this pointer. Later, `password_clear_cmd_handler()` (line 508) attempts to free the same
pointer again, causing double-free.
     ```

● Bash(git show fbaaf8ba0 grub-core/Makefile.core.def | grep -B3 -A8 "video_bochs\|video_cirrus")
  ⎿  Author: Andrew Hamilton <adhamilt@gmail.com>
     Date:   Mon Sep 29 21:23:40 2025 -0500

         build: Disable building video_bochs/cirrus for EFI x86

         Disable building video_bochs and video_cirrus for EFI x86 platforms (i386_efi
         and x86_64_efi). For x86 EFI platforms efi_gop or efi_uga should provide the
         video support. Currently, in EFI platforms there is a risk that both the
         legacy device is present and the EFI video interface - which can cause
         unexpected video corruption when a mix of the two interfaces are used. This
         can happen when all_video is loaded for simplicity in a GRUB config.

         Fixes: https://savannah.gnu.org/bugs/index.php?66200

     --
     +++ b/grub-core/Makefile.core.def
     @@ -2194,13 +2194,21 @@ module = {
      module = {
        name = video_cirrus;
        x86 = video/cirrus.c;
     -  enable = x86;
     +  enable = i386_pc;
     +  enable = i386_qemu;
     +  enable = i386_coreboot;
     +  enable = i386_multiboot;
     +  enable = i386_ieee1275;
      };

      module = {
        name = video_bochs;
        x86 = video/bochs.c;
     -  enable = x86;
     +  enable = i386_pc;
     +  enable = i386_qemu;
     +  enable = i386_coreboot;
     +  enable = i386_multiboot;
     +  enable = i386_ieee1275;
      };

● Bash(git show 404061969 --stat)
  ⎿  commit 404061969e47875240f15ab1f143aa59a822d1d6
     Author: Renaud Métrich <rmetrich@redhat.com>
     Date:   Wed Nov 26 15:34:58 2025 +0100

         efi: new 'connectefi' command

         When network booting is used, trying to chainload to the local disk
         (which is used in deployment tools such as Red Hat Satellite) may fail
         when searching for the boot loader, e.g. /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi:
         the boot loader file is listed, but not readable, because UEFI DISK I/O
         and/or SCSI DISK I/O devices are not connected.
         Sometimes devices are connected but not the partition of the disk
         matching $prefix, causing partition to not be found by"chainloader".

         This issue was seen already on:
         - VMWare systems with efi.quickboot enabled (which is the default)
         - QEMU/KVM when network booting and not enabling any disk
         - Physical hardware such as Cisco and HPE systems

         Typical chainload configuration used:
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------
         unset prefix
         search --file --set=prefix /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         if [ -n "$prefix" ]; then
           chainloader ($prefix)/EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         ...
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------

         This patch introduces a new "connectefi pciroot|disk|all" command which
         recursively connects all EFI devices starting from a given controller
         type:
         - if 'pciroot' is specified, recursion is performed for all PCI root
           handles
         - if 'disk' is specified, recursion is performed for all SCSI and DISK
           I/O handles (recommended usage to avoid connecting unwanted handles
           which may impact Grub performances)
         - if 'all' is specified, recursion is performed on all handles (not
           recommended since it may heavily impact Grub performances)

         Typical grub.cfg snippet would then be:
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------
         connectefi disk
         unset prefix
         search --file --set=prefix /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         if [ -n "$prefix" ]; then
           chainloader ($prefix)/EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         ...
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------

         The code is easily extensible to handle other arguments in the future if
         needed.

         Signed-off-by: Renaud Métrich <rmetrich@redhat.com>

      docs/grub.texi                      |  33 ++++++
      grub-core/Makefile.core.def         |   6 +
      grub-core/commands/efi/connectefi.c | 227 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
      grub-core/disk/efi/efidisk.c        |  13 +++
      include/grub/efi/disk.h             |   2 +
      5 files changed, 281 insertions(+)

∴ Thinking…

         This patch introduces a new "connectefi pciroot|disk|all" command which
         recursively connects all EFI devices starting from a given controller
         type:
         - if 'pciroot' is specified, recursion is performed for all PCI root
           handles
         - if 'disk' is specified, recursion is performed for all SCSI and DISK
           I/O handles (recommended usage to avoid connecting unwanted handles
           which may impact Grub performances)
         - if 'all' is specified, recursion is performed on all handles (not
           recommended since it may heavily impact Grub performances)

         Typical grub.cfg snippet would then be:
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------
         connectefi disk
         unset prefix
         search --file --set=prefix /EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         if [ -n "$prefix" ]; then
           chainloader ($prefix)/EFI/redhat/shimx64.efi
         ...
         -------- 8< ---------------- 8< ---------------- 8< --------

         The code is easily extensible to handle other arguments in the future if
         needed.

         Signed-off-by: Renaud Métrich <rmetrich@redhat.com>

     +typedef enum search_item_flag search_item_flag_t;
     +
     +struct search_item

     +static bool
     +is_in_list (grub_efi_handle_t handle, grub_efi_handle_list_t *handles)
     +{
     +  grub_efi_handle_list_t *e;
     +
     +  FOR_LIST_ELEMENTS (e, handles)
     +    if (e->handle == handle)
     +      return true;
     +
     +  return false;
     +}
     +
     +static void
     +free_handle_list (grub_efi_handle_list_t **handles_p)
     +{
     +  grub_efi_handle_list_t *e, *n;
     +
     +  FOR_LIST_ELEMENTS_SAFE (e, n, *handles_p)
     +    grub_free (e);
     +
     +  *handles_p = NULL;
     +}

     +static grub_err_t
     +grub_cmd_connectefi (grub_command_t cmd __attribute__ ((unused)),
     +		     int argc, char **args)
     +{

     +    };
     +  search_item_t disk_items[] =
     +    {
     +      { GRUB_EFI_PCI_ROOT_IO_GUID, "PCI root", SEARCH_NONE },
     +      { GRUB_EFI_PCI_IO_GUID, "PCI", SEARCH_LOOP },
     +      { GRUB_EFI_SCSI_IO_PROTOCOL_GUID, "SCSI I/O", SEARCH_RECURSIVE },
     +      { GRUB_EFI_DISK_IO_GUID, "DISK I/O", SEARCH_RECURSIVE }
     +    };
     +  search_item_t *items = NULL;
     +  int i, nitems = 0;

     +  bool reenumerate_disks = false;
     +  grub_efi_handle_list_t *already_handled = NULL;
     +  grub_efi_handle_t *handles = NULL;

     +  if (argc != 1)
     +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("one argument expected"));

     +    {
     +      items = pciroot_items;
     +      nitems = ARRAY_SIZE (pciroot_items);
     +    }
     +  else if (grub_strcmp (args[0], "disk") == 0)
     +    {
     +      items = disk_items;
     +      nitems = ARRAY_SIZE (disk_items);
     +    }

     +	    break; /* proceed to next item */

     +	  for (j = 0; j < num_handles; j++)
     +	    {
     +	      grub_efi_handle_t handle = handles[j];
     +	      grub_efi_status_t status;
     +	      grub_efi_handle_list_t *item;

     +	      /* Skip already handled handles */
     +	      if (is_in_list (handle, already_handled))
     +		{
     +		  grub_dprintf ("efi", "  handle %p: already processed\n", handle);
     +		  continue;

     +			handle, NULL, NULL,

     +		  continue;

     +			handle, NULL, NULL,

     +		  grub_dprintf ("efi", "  handle %p: connected\n", handle);
     +		}
     +	      else
     +		grub_dprintf ("efi", "  handle %p: failed to connect (%"
     +			      PRIxGRUB_EFI_UINTN_T ")\n", handle, status);
     +
     +	      item = grub_malloc (sizeof (*item));
     +	      if (item == NULL)
     +		{
     +		  err = grub_errno;
     +		  goto fail; /* fatal */
     +		}
     +	      grub_list_push (GRUB_AS_LIST_P (&already_handled), GRUB_AS_LIST (item));
     +	      item->handle = handle;
     +	    }
     +
     +	  grub_free (handles);
     +	  handles = NULL;
     +	}
     +      while (continue_search == true);
     +
     +      /* We are done with this item, proceed to next one */
     +      free_handle_list (&already_handled);
     +    }
     +
     + fail:
     +  grub_free (handles);
     +  free_handle_list (&already_handled);
     +
     +  if (reenumerate_disks)
     +    grub_efidisk_reenumerate_disks ();
     +
     +  return err;

     +				  " and DISK I/O EFI handles recursively."
     +				  " If 'all' is specified, connect all"
     +				  " EFI handles recursively."));
     +}
     +
     +GRUB_MOD_FINI(connectefi)
     +{
     +  grub_unregister_command (cmd);
     +}

∴ Thinking…

  Let me analyze this code carefully for any issues:

  1. Memory handling: The code allocates handles via grub_efi_locate_handle() and frees it properly with grub_free(handles) at the end of the loop and in the
  fail label. Good.
  2. Error handling: When grub_malloc fails at line ~33, it sets err and goes to fail label, which properly cleans up both handles and already_handled. Good.
  3. Resource cleanup: The fail label calls both grub_free(handles) and free_handle_list(&already_handled). Good.
  4. Recursion limit: The code has a max_loop_depth check of 100 to prevent infinite loops. Good.
  5. Logic issue I need to check: Looking at the nested loops and the handles pointer usage...

  Wait, I see a potential issue:

  In the main for loop (line ~33 of my earlier output), we have:
  for (i = 0; i < nitems; i++)
  {
    do
    {
      handles = grub_efi_locate_handle(...);  // Allocates handles

      if (handles == NULL)
        break; /* proceed to next item */

      for (j = 0; j < num_handles; j++)
      {
        // Process handles
        if (malloc fails)
        {
          goto fail;  // Good - handles will be freed at fail label
        }
      }

      grub_free (handles);  // Frees handles at end of inner for loop
      handles = NULL;
    }
    while (continue_search == true);

    /* We are done with this item, proceed to next one */
    free_handle_list (&already_handled);
  }

  Wait, there's an issue here.

  After each item type completes its do-while loop, free_handle_list(&already_handled) clears the tracking list and the outer loop moves to the next item with
  a fresh set of handles to process. This appears intentional—each item type (PCI root, PCI, SCSI I/O, DISK I/O) gets its own pass through the handle discovery
   process, and the already-processed list is reset between iterations.

  But this raises a question: should handles processed during the PCI phase be skipped when we reach the DISK I/O phase? The current design allows the same
  handle to be connected multiple times across different item types. Looking at the disk_items array structure with its sequence of GUIDs and search strategies
   suggests each phase serves a distinct purpose in building the device hierarchy.

  When handles == NULL from a failed locate operation, the break statement exits the loop and moves to the next item. The subsequent grub_free(handles) call
  safely handles the NULL pointer since the memory allocator should account for this.

  The resource management appears sound, error handling is appropriate, and clearing already_handled between iterations makes sense since each protocol GUID
  targets different handle types. I don't identify any actual bugs in this implementation.

  recursion limit prevents infinite loops (max_loop_depth=100), and clearing already_handled between items is
  correct since different items use different GUIDs. Review "No issues found" is accurate.

∴ Thinking…

  Let me analyze this change:

  Before:```c
  if (!*pos)
    *pos = grub_calloc (linep->len + 1, sizeof (**pos));

  After:
  ```c
  if (*pos == NULL)
    {
      *pos = grub_calloc (linep->len + 1, sizeof (**pos));
      if (*pos == NULL)
        return;
    }

  This adds a NULL check after the grub_calloc call. If the allocation fails, the function returns early to avoid dereferencing a NULL pointer.

  The change looks correct. There are no issues with:
  1. The logic - it properly checks for allocation failure
  2. Resource management - returning early is fine, as there's no cleanup needed at this point
  3. Error handling - returning is appropriate for update_screen which is a void function

     +  grub_printf_ (N_("Retrieving MFA key 1 from '%s' key protector\n"), mfa_ctx.protector1);

     +  *key = grub_malloc (*key_size);
     +  if (*key == NULL)
     +    {
     +      err = GRUB_ERR_OUT_OF_MEMORY;
     --

     +  };

● Bash(git show efd303f3a grub-core/commands/mfa.c | grep -A50 "^+mfa_recover_key")
  ⎿  +mfa_recover_key (grub_uint8_t **key, grub_size_t *key_size)
     +{
     +  grub_uint8_t *p1_key = NULL, *p2_key = NULL;
     +  grub_size_t p1_size, p2_size, total_size;
     +  grub_err_t err;
     +
     +  if (mfa_ctx.protector1 == NULL || mfa_ctx.protector2 == NULL)
     +    return grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT, N_("MFA key protector(s) not set"));
     +
     +  grub_printf_ (N_("Retrieving MFA key 1 from '%s' key protector\n"), mfa_ctx.protector1);
     +  err = grub_key_protector_recover_key (mfa_ctx.protector1, &p1_key, &p1_size);
     +  if (err != GRUB_ERR_NONE)
     +    goto error;
     +
     +  grub_printf_ (N_("Retrieving MFA key 2 from '%s' key protector\n"), mfa_ctx.protector2);
     +  err = grub_key_protector_recover_key (mfa_ctx.protector2, &p2_key, &p2_size);
     +  if (err != GRUB_ERR_NONE)
     +    goto error;
     +
     +  total_size = p1_size + p2_size;
     +  if (total_size < p1_size || total_size < p2_size || total_size == 0)
     +    {
     +      err = grub_error (GRUB_ERR_BAD_NUMBER, N_("invalid MFA key size"));
     +      goto error;
     +    }
     +
     +  *key_size = total_size;
     +  *key = grub_malloc (*key_size);
     +  if (*key == NULL)
     +    {
     +      err = GRUB_ERR_OUT_OF_MEMORY;
     +      goto error;
     +    }
     +
     +  grub_memcpy (*key, p1_key, p1_size);
     +  grub_memcpy (*key + p1_size, p2_key, p2_size);
     +
     +  err = GRUB_ERR_NONE;
     +
     +error:
     +  /* Erase the intermediate keys */
     +  if (p1_key != NULL)
     +    {
     +      grub_memset (p1_key, 0, p1_size);
     +      grub_free (p1_key);
     +    }
     +  if (p2_key != NULL)
     +    {
     +      grub_memset (p2_key, 0, p2_size);
     +      grub_free (p2_key);
     +    }

∴ Thinking…

● Bash(git show 19b605894)
  ⎿  commit 19b605894c0a254869afa1e7d6c90ad10c649f0b
     Author: Glenn Washburn <development@efficientek.com>
     Date:   Fri Jan 23 20:06:46 2026 -0600

         libgcrypt: Re-encode versioninfo.rc.in to UTF8 from latin-1

         Files that are not UTF8 encoded may be assumed to be so by some tools,
         like git send-email. This can inturn cause confusion by tools further
         down the line. To avoid this all GRUB test files should be UTF8
         encoded.

         Signed-off-by: Glenn Washburn <development@efficientek.com>

     diff --git a/grub-core/lib/libgcrypt/src/versioninfo.rc.in b/grub-core/lib/libgcrypt/src/versioninfo.rc.in

     +++ b/grub-core/lib/libgcrypt/src/versioninfo.rc.in

● Bash(git show 384f82558)
  ⎿  commit 384f825589a8d20260629f62fb208193779e673b
     Author: Avnish Chouhan <avnish@linux.ibm.com>
     Date:   Tue Jan 27 19:18:14 2026 +0530

         Mandatory install device check for PowerPC

         This patch adds a check on install_device while installing grub for PowerPC.
         If install_device is not mentioned in grub2-install and machine is detected
         as PowerPC, the error will be thrown and it will terminates the grub2-install
         operation. Running grub2-install on PowerPC without the install_device may
         result in bootlist corruption. When no install device is specified, it attempts
         to load images from the filesystem, which leads to nvram bootlist corruption.
         The idea is to fail the operation and avoid creating the invalid boot entry.

         Signed-off-by: Avnish Chouhan <avnish@linux.ibm.com>

     diff --git a/util/grub-install.c b/util/grub-install.c
     index 060246589..896ea822a 100644
     --- a/util/grub-install.c
     +++ b/util/grub-install.c
     @@ -1289,6 +1289,19 @@ main (int argc, char *argv[])
      	      is_prep = 0;
      	    }
      	}
     +#if defined(__powerpc__)
     +     else
     +       {
     +         /*
     +	   * As the machine has been detected as PowerPC and not a PowerMac. We need to check
     +	   * whether the install_device has been mentioned while installing. If no device has been
     +	   * mentioned, we need to exit and mark it as an error as the install_device is required for
     +	   * PowerPC installation. An installation with no device mentioned may lead to corruptions.
     +	   */
     +          if (!install_device)
     +            grub_util_error ("%s", _("install device isn't specified, required for PowerPC"));
     +       }
     +#endif /* __powerpc__ */
          }

        size_t ndev = 0;

∴ Thinking…

  Let me analyze this change:The code adds a check specifically for PowerPC platforms:1. It's wrapped in #if defined(__powerpc__)
  2. It's in an else clause (so it only runs when certain other conditions aren't met)3. It checks if install_device is NULL
  4. If NULL, it calls grub_util_error() to terminate

  Let me check the context to understand the full flow. Let me see what comes before this else clause...

● Bash(git show 1182632db --stat)
  ⎿  commit 1182632db09bbbce3a3e05f64a84e335f408f141
     Author: Khalid Ali <khaliidcaliy@gmail.com>
     Date:   Mon Feb 9 07:59:48 2026 +0000

         efi: Always close protocols after usage

         Currently grub doesn't close used protocols, this causes internal
         firmware resource leak.

         So make sure to close all protocols after usage.

         Signed-off-by: Khalid Ali <khaliidcaliy@gmail.com>

      grub-core/commands/bli.c           | 1 +

  ⎿  f42c040ed zfs: fix root dataset detection on encrypted pools

● Bash(git show f42c040ed)
  ⎿  commit f42c040eddd3ec1e2610c2311bec89bc696de45c
     Author: Christian Schoenebeck <linux_oss@crudebyte.com>
     Date:   Mon Feb 9 19:56:00 2026 +0100

         zfs: fix root dataset detection on encrypted pools

         grub-probe fails on encrypted zfs pools, causing $rpool variable
         to be empty. Therefore check variables $rpool and $bootfs to
         be both non-empty strings before assembling LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE.

         If one of the two variables is empty, use "zfs list" as fallback
         for detecting the zfs root dataset instead.

         Signed-off-by: Christian Schoenebeck <linux_oss@crudebyte.com>

     diff --git a/util/grub.d/10_linux.in b/util/grub.d/10_linux.in
     index 1233b112b..0665ed282 100644
     --- a/util/grub.d/10_linux.in
     +++ b/util/grub.d/10_linux.in
     @@ -75,7 +75,13 @@ case x"$GRUB_FS" in
          xzfs)
      	rpool=`${grub_probe} --device ${GRUB_DEVICE} --target=fs_label 2>/dev/null || zdb -l ${GRUB_DEVICE} | awk -F \' '/ name/ { print $2 }'`
      	bootfs="`make_system_path_relative_to_its_root / | sed -e "s,@$,,"`"
     -	LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE="ZFS=${rpool}${bootfs%/}"
     +	# grub-probe fails on encrypted zfs pools, use zfs list as fallback
     +	if [ -n "$rpool" ] && [ -n "$bootfs" ]; then
     +	    LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE="ZFS=${rpool}${bootfs%/}"
     +	else
     +	    root_dataset=`zfs list -Ho mountpoint,name | grep '^/[[:space:]]' | tail -n1 | cut -f 2 2>/dev/null || true`
     +	    LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE="ZFS=${root_dataset}"
     +	fi
      	;;
      esac

∴ Thinking…

  Let me analyze this change:Before:bash LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE="ZFS=${rpool}${bootfs%/}"

  After:```bash
  grub-probe fails on encrypted zfs pools, use zfs list as fallback

  if [ -n "$rpool" ] && [ -n "$bootfs" ]; then
      LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE="ZFS=${rpool}${bootfs%/}"else
      root_dataset=zfs list -Ho mountpoint,name | grep '^/[[:space:]]' | tail -n1 | cut -f 2 2>/dev/null || true    LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE="ZFS=${root_dataset}"
  fi

  The change adds a check to see if both `$rpool` and `$bootfs` are non-empty before using them. If either is empty (as happens with encrypted pools), it falls
   back to using `zfs list` to find the root dataset.

  Issues to check:1. **Logic**: The fallback command looks for datasets with mountpoint `/`, takes the last one (`tail -n1`), and extracts the dataset name
  (`cut -f 2`). This seems reasonable.2. **Error handling**: The command has `|| true` at the end to prevent errors from propagating. Good.3. **Edge case**:
  What if `zfs list` also fails or returns empty? Then `root_dataset` will be empty and `LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE` will be "ZFS=" which might cause issues.

  Actually, looking at this more carefully, if `zfs list` fails or returns empty, we'd have `LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE="ZFS="` which is probably not ideal. It might be
   better to check if `root_dataset` is non-empty before using it. But this is an edge case and the current code is probably fine for most use cases.

  However, this is a shell script fix, not a C code fix. The logic seems sound for the described use case. The review says "No issues found" which is
  reasonable - while there's a potential edge case (zfs list failing), the fix addresses the main problem described in the commit message.

  pools case), falls back to zfs list -Ho mountpoint,name | grep '^/[[:space:]]' | tail -n1 | cut -f 2. Logic is
  sound, error handling includes || true to prevent error propagation. Minor edge case: if fallback also fails,
  LINUX_ROOT_DEVICE would be "ZFS=" but this is acceptable given the fix addresses the main issue. Review "No
  issues found" is reasonable.

         efi: Drop support for UGA Draw and Console Control protocols

         Let's stop GRUB from becoming a software museum and drop support for
         deprecated EFI protocols.

         - UGA Draw protocol was deprecated in UEFI 2.0 in 2006.
         - Console Control protocol was an implementation detail of
           the deprecated Intel EDK (not EDK II).

         Signed-off-by: Mate Kukri <mate.kukri@canonical.com>

     diff --git a/grub-core/normal/menu_entry.c b/grub-core/normal/menu_entry.c

     @@ -686,6 +686,48 @@ backward_char (struct screen *screen, int update)
        return 1;
      }

     +static int
     +forward_word (struct screen *screen, int update)
     +{
     +  int prev_column = screen->column;
     +  struct line *linep;
     +
     +  linep = screen->lines + screen->line;
     +  while (screen->column < linep->len && grub_isspace (linep->buf[screen->column]))
     +    ++screen->column;
     +  while (screen->column < linep->len && grub_isalnum (linep->buf[screen->column]))
     +    ++screen->column;
     +  if (prev_column == screen->column)
     +    {
     +      if (! forward_char (screen, 0))
     +	return 0;
     +    }
     +  if (update)
     +    update_screen_all (screen, screen->num_lines, 0, 0, 0, NO_LINE);
     +  return 1;
     +}
     +
     +static int
     +backward_word (struct screen *screen, int update)
     +{
     +  int prev_column = screen->column;
     +  struct line *linep;
     +
     +  linep = screen->lines + screen->line;
     +  while (screen->column > 0 && grub_isspace (linep->buf[screen->column - 1]))
     +    screen->column--;
     +  while (screen->column > 0 && grub_isalnum (linep->buf[screen->column - 1]))
     +    screen->column--;
     +  if (prev_column == screen->column)
     +    {
     +      if (! backward_char (screen, 0))
     +	return 0;
     +    }
     +  if (update)
     +    update_screen_all (screen, screen->num_lines, 0, 0, 0, NO_LINE);
     +  return 1;
     +}
     +
      static int
      previous_line (struct screen *screen, int update)
      {
     @@ -869,6 +911,88 @@ backward_delete_char (struct screen *screen, int update)
        return 1;
      }

     +static int
     +delete_word (struct screen *screen, int swap_whitespace, int update)
     +{
     +  struct line *linep;
     +  unsigned i;
     +  int del;
     +
     +  linep = screen->lines + screen->line;
     +  for (i = 0; i < screen->nterms; i++)
     +    screen->terms[i].orig_num =
     +      get_logical_num_lines (linep, &screen->terms[i]);
     +
     +  del = 0;
     +  if (swap_whitespace)
     +    {
     +      while (screen->column + del < linep->len
     +	     && grub_isalnum (linep->buf[screen->column + del]))
     +	del++;
     +      while (screen->column + del < linep->len
     +	     && grub_isspace (linep->buf[screen->column + del]))
     +	del++;
     +    }
     +  else
     +    {
     +      while (screen->column + del < linep->len
     +	     && grub_isspace (linep->buf[screen->column + del]))
     +	del++;
     +      while (screen->column + del < linep->len
     +	     && grub_isalnum (linep->buf[screen->column + del]))
     +	del++;
     +    }

∴ Thinking…

  function and the key bindings.

∴ Thinking…

  Now I can see the complete implementation. Let me analyze for any bugs:1. forward_word(): - Skips whitespace first, then alphanumeric characters   - If
  column doesn't change, calls forward_char()   - Looks safe - checks screen->column < linep->len before accessing buffer2. backward_word():
  - Skips whitespace backwards, then alphanumeric backwards
  - Uses screen->column - 1 as index, checks screen->column > 0 first
  - Looks safe
  3. delete_word():   - Has a swap_whitespace parameter to control order of deletion   - Uses screen->column + del as index, checks screen->column + del <
  linep->len
    - Looks safe
  4. backward_delete_word():   - Checks if at start of buffer, returns early
    - Calls backward_word() then delete_word()   - Looks safe
  5. Key bindings:
    - Ctrl+Left: backward_word
    - Ctrl+Right: forward_word
    - Ctrl+W / Alt+Backspace: backward_delete_word
    - Ctrl+Delete: delete_word
    - All properly check return values and goto fail on error
    - Looks safeAll the boundary checks look correct. The functions check array bounds before accessing buffers. The logic for skipping whitespace and
  alphanumeric characters is correct. I don't see any bugs in this implementation.

● Bash(git show b90405a81 --stat)
  ⎿  commit b90405a81759dcde0e883471e529e20a161eb86f
     Author: Mate Kukri <mate.kukri@canonical.com>
     Date:   Wed Feb 11 10:21:00 2026 +0000

         normal/cmdline: Add support for jump/delete word shortcuts

         These work similarly to bash/readline. Word here means a possible run of
         whitespace plus a run of alphanumeric characters (viewed from the approach
         direction relative to the cursor).

         Ctrl+left/right: jump word left or right
         Ctrl+delete: delete word right of the cursor
         Ctrl+w, Alt+backspace: delete word left of the cursor

         Signed-off-by: Mate Kukri <mate.kukri@canonical.com>

      grub-core/normal/cmdline.c | 60 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
      1 file changed, 60 insertions(+)

∴ Thinking…

  Let me view the actual implementation to check for bugs. This is 60 lines added, similar pattern to the menu entry editor version.

● Bash(git show b90405a81 grub-core/normal/cmdline.c)
  ⎿  commit b90405a81759dcde0e883471e529e20a161eb86f
     Author: Mate Kukri <mate.kukri@canonical.com>
     Date:   Wed Feb 11 10:21:00 2026 +0000

         normal/cmdline: Add support for jump/delete word shortcuts

         These work similarly to bash/readline. Word here means a possible run of
         whitespace plus a run of alphanumeric characters (viewed from the approach
         direction relative to the cursor).

         Ctrl+left/right: jump word left or right
         Ctrl+delete: delete word right of the cursor
         Ctrl+w, Alt+backspace: delete word left of the cursor

         Signed-off-by: Mate Kukri <mate.kukri@canonical.com>

     diff --git a/grub-core/normal/cmdline.c b/grub-core/normal/cmdline.c
     index 0875250be..39ee6ea16 100644
     --- a/grub-core/normal/cmdline.c
     +++ b/grub-core/normal/cmdline.c
     @@ -355,6 +355,49 @@ cl_insert (struct cmdline_term *cl_terms, unsigned nterms,
          }
      }

     +static void
     +skip_word_rev (struct cmdline_term *cl_terms, unsigned nterms,
     +	       grub_uint32_t *buf, grub_size_t *lpos,
     +	       grub_size_t *llen, int delete)
     +{
     +  grub_size_t endpos = *lpos;
     +  /* trailing whitespace sequence */
     +  while (*lpos > 0 && grub_isspace(buf[*lpos - 1]))
     +    --*lpos;
     +  /* alphanumeric sequence */
     +  while (*lpos > 0 && grub_isalnum(buf[*lpos - 1]))
     +    --*lpos;
     +  /* skip one char if the above logic yielded nothing and we can */
     +  if (*lpos > 0 && endpos - *lpos == 0)
     +    --*lpos;
     +  cl_set_pos_all (cl_terms, nterms, *lpos);
     +  if (delete)
     +    cl_delete (cl_terms, nterms, buf, *lpos, llen, endpos - *lpos);
     +}
     +
     +static void
     +skip_word_fwd (struct cmdline_term *cl_terms, unsigned nterms,
     +	       grub_uint32_t *buf, grub_size_t *lpos,
     +	       grub_size_t *llen, int delete)
     +{
     +  grub_size_t endpos = *lpos;
     +  /* leading whitespace sequence */
     +  while (endpos < *llen && grub_isspace(buf[endpos]))
     +    ++endpos;
     +  /* alphanumeric sequence */
     +  while (endpos < *llen && grub_isalnum(buf[endpos]))
     +    ++endpos;
     +  /* skip one char if the above logic yielded nothing and we can */
     +  if (endpos < *llen && endpos - *lpos == 0)
     +    ++endpos;
     +  if (delete)
     +    cl_delete (cl_terms, nterms, buf, *lpos, llen, endpos - *lpos);
     +  else
     +    {
     +      *lpos = endpos;
     +      cl_set_pos_all (cl_terms, nterms, *lpos);
     +    }
     +}

      /* Get a command-line. If ESC is pushed, return zero,
         otherwise return command line.  */
     @@ -648,6 +691,23 @@ grub_cmdline_get (const char *prompt_translated)
      	    cl_insert (cl_terms, nterms, &lpos, &llen, &max_len, &buf, kill_buf);
      	  break;

     +	case GRUB_TERM_CTRL | GRUB_TERM_KEY_LEFT:
     +	  skip_word_rev (cl_terms, nterms, buf, &lpos, &llen, 0);
     +	  break;
     +
     +	case GRUB_TERM_CTRL | GRUB_TERM_KEY_RIGHT:
     +	  skip_word_fwd (cl_terms, nterms, buf, &lpos, &llen, 0);
     +	  break;
     +
     +	case GRUB_TERM_CTRL | 'w':
     +	case GRUB_TERM_ALT | GRUB_TERM_BACKSPACE:
     +	  skip_word_rev (cl_terms, nterms, buf, &lpos, &llen, 1);
     +	  break;
     +
     +	case GRUB_TERM_CTRL | GRUB_TERM_KEY_DC:
     +	  skip_word_fwd (cl_terms, nterms, buf, &lpos, &llen, 1);
     +	  break;
     +
      	case GRUB_TERM_ESC:
      	  grub_free (cl_terms);
      	  grub_free (buf);

∴ Thinking…

     +int
     +add_filename_to_pile (char *filename, struct ofpath_files_list_root* root)
     +{
     +  struct ofpath_files_list_node* file;
     +
     +  file = malloc (sizeof (struct ofpath_files_list_node));
     +  if (!file)
     +    return -1;
     +
     +  file->filename = malloc (sizeof (char) * 1024);
     +  if (!file->filename)
     +    {
     +      free (file);
     +      return -1;
     +    }
     +
     +  grub_strcpy (file->filename, filename);
     +  if (root->first == NULL)
     +    {
     +      root->items = 1;
     +      root->first = file;
     +      file->next = NULL;
     +    }
     +  else
     +    {
     +      root->items++;
     +      file->next = root->first;
     +      root->first = file;
     +    }
     +
     +  return 0;
     +}
     +
     +void
     +find_file (char* filename, char* directory, struct ofpath_files_list_root* root, int max_depth, int depth)
     +{
     +  struct dirent *ep;
     +  struct stat statbuf;
     +  DIR *dp;
     +  int ret_val=0;
     +  char* full_path;
     +
     +  if (depth > max_depth)
     +    {
     +      return;
     +    }
     +
     +  if ((dp = opendir (directory)) == NULL)
     +    {
     +      return;
     +    }
     +
     +  full_path = malloc (1024 * sizeof (char));
     +  if (!full_path)
     +    return;
     +
     +  while ((ep = readdir(dp)) != NULL)
     +    {
     +      snprintf (full_path, 1024, "%s/%s", directory, ep->d_name);
     +      lstat (full_path, &statbuf);
     +
     +      if (S_ISLNK (statbuf.st_mode))
     +        {
     +          continue;
     +        }
     +
     +      if (!strcmp (ep->d_name, ".") || !strcmp(ep->d_name, ".."))
     +        {
     +          continue;
     +        }
     +
     +      if (!strcmp (ep->d_name, filename))
     +        {
     +          ret_val = add_filename_to_pile (full_path, root);
     +          if (ret_val == -1)
     +            continue;
     +        }
     +
     +      find_file (filename, full_path, root, max_depth, depth+1);
     +    }
     +
     +  free (full_path);
     +  closedir (dp);
     +}
     +

      of_path_of_vdisk(const char *sys_devname __attribute__((unused)),
      		 const char *device,
     @@ -382,7 +468,244 @@ of_fc_port_name (const char *path, const char *subpath, char *port_name)
        free (basepath);
      }

     +      free (node->filename);
     +      free (node);
     +      node = next;

     +  return;

∴ Thinking…

  This code shows:

  1. New data structures for file list management (linked list)
  2. File system traversal using opendir(), readdir(), lstat()
  3. Memory allocation with malloc() and free() (not grub_malloc/grub_free)
  4. Use of grub_strcpy which could be unsafe if not properly bounded
  5. Recursive directory search with depth limiting
  6. PowerPC/SCSI/FibreChannel specific code dealing with WWPNs and device discovery

  Looking at line like:
  file->filename = malloc (sizeof (char) * 1024);
  ...
  grub_strcpy (file->filename, filename);

  This could be unsafe if filename is longer than 1024 bytes.

  And:
  full_path = malloc (1024 * sizeof (char));
  ...
  snprintf (full_path, 1024, "%s/%s", directory, ep->d_name);

  snprintf is safe, but the 1024 limit could be problematic for deep paths.

  This is complex, platform-specific code dealing with:
  - PowerPC OpenFirmware IEEE1275 interfaces
  - NVMe over Fibre Channel protocol
  - Device path translation
  - File system traversal for device discovery

  The review's assessment that "Cannot thoroughly review due to complexity and platform-specific nature. Recommend testing on target hardware" is absolutely
  reasonable because:

  1. Platform-specific: Only works on PowerPC/IEEE1275 systems with NVMeoFC hardware
  2. Complex protocol: Involves firmware interfaces, WWPN/LUN handling, NVMe namespaces
  3. Requires hardware: Can't be tested without actual NVMeoFC hardware on PowerPC
  4. Large change: 861 insertions across 5 files
  5. Specialized knowledge: Requires understanding of Fibre Channel, NVMe, and OpenFirmware

  6. Validation constraints: Without access to the target hardware and specialized expertise, attempting to identify specific bugs would be speculative rather
  than substantive

● Bash(git show 7bced2906 --stat)
  ⎿  commit 7bced290691c1a63310b2fb20b9b452c92b9d355
     Author: Mikhail Lappo <miklelappo@gmail.com>
     Date:   Wed Feb 25 14:06:57 2026 +0100

         loader/efi/fdt: Ensure FDT blob is 8-byte aligned

         libfdt requires the FDT blob to be 8-byte aligned. The alignment is
         validated in fdt_check_header(), and misaligned blobs may be rejected
         or cause failures on architectures that enforce strict alignment.

         Currently the blob is allocated with grub_malloc(), which does not
         guarantee 8-byte alignment. Replace it with grub_memalign(8, size)
         to ensure the required alignment.

         Signed-off-by: Mikhail Lappo <miklelappo@gmail.com>

      grub-core/loader/efi/fdt.c | 3 ++-
      1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

∴ Thinking…

  Good, one file changed. Let me see the actual diff to verify the grub_malloc() to grub_memalign() change.

  - Adds #if !defined(GRUB_MACHINE_EMU) guards around:  - The PKS header include

  Changes in include/grub/powerpc/ieee1275/platform_keystore.h:
  - Removes the #if defined(__powerpc__) conditional around function declarations
  - Removes the stub implementations that returned NULL/did nothing
  - This makes the functions always declared (header-only), and the actual definitions are in the .c file which is only compiled for PowerPC IEEE1275
  platform (not EMU)

  This works together with !80 which added && !(GRUB_MACHINE_EMU) condition in the same header file. Together they fully exclude PKS support when
  building grub-emu, fixing the linker errors.
